one_paragraph_summary: Published October 23, 2023 in the Inquirer Opinion as a column-version of his valedictory address as head of the Philippine delegation to the ASEAN Law Association (ALA) General Assembly in Kuala Lumpur on October 17-22, 2023 (graced by Malaysian PM Anwar Ibrahim), this piece compresses the speech for newspaper readers. CJP announces the handover of the ALA Philippines chairmanship to incumbent Philippine CJ Alexander G. Gesmundo effective January 1, 2024 Ś a compromise arising from Gesmundo's preoccupation with the SC's 'Strategic Plan for Judicial Innovation' for 'the Age of Artificial Intelligence' and CJP's self-deprecating admission that he was 'an ancient relic who has overstayed' since 2015. He names the seven other SC justices in the 50-person Philippine delegation. He frames ASEAN's 40-year history as the practical demonstration that consensus and rule of law Ś explicitly NOT mob rule, propaganda, falsehood, or nuclear weapons, and not even 'gray tactics' Ś are the only durable regional methods. He self-quotes his February 26, 2015 ALA address on truth as eternal and unbounded, reports FLP's progress (83 scholarships, 16 chairs, 27 dissertation winners, 10 ESMEL fellowships), and announces the two ultimate projects (Center for Liberty and Prosperity with AI-powered Museum, and the multibillion-peso Prosperity Fund), which he hopes to complete 'before I leave this earth.'

one_paragraph_summary: Published January 16, 2023, this column reads the Supreme Court's December 2022 12-2-1 decision (penned by Justice Samuel H. Gaerlan) voiding the Tripartite Agreement for Joint Marine Seismic Undertaking (JMSU Ś among China National Offshore Oil, Vietnam Oil and Gas, and Philippine National Oil) as a 'subtle reminder' to the Philippine Joint Development panel negotiating with China on EEZ resources. The Court applied substance-over-form: although the JMSU labeled the work 'pre-exploration activities,' the intent and aim made it exploration. Two constitutional safeguards from Article XII Section 2 are non-negotiable: full state control and supervision, plus the 60-40 Filipino-citizenship requirement. CJP argues the draft MOU's February 2022 language Ś 'oil and gas operations shall be subject to the laws of the Philippines,' with a non-renunciation clause Ś already satisfies these safeguards, so a Chinese refusal to finalize would expose bad faith: 'dribbling the ball and refusing to take a shot, a wasteful tactic already rejected in basketball-crazy Philippines.' If procrastination continues, the President is justified in turning to the United States Ś with a US-compliant MOU justifying permanent 24/7 American armed-forces deployment in the Philippine EEZ under the Mutual Defense Treaty. He answers Eddie Yap's text question about the 15-year ruling delay only briefly, deferring full discussion. He closes with condolences for Ambassador Manuel M. Lopez.

one_paragraph_summary: Panganiban cuts through the public noise on the indictment, arrest, chartered-jet airlift, and Netherlands detention of former President Rodrigo R. Duterte ├óŌé¼ŌĆØ comments 'essayed, broadcasted, debated, and gossiped ad nauseam' ├óŌé¼ŌĆØ to identify what he believes is the pivotal legal issue: the jurisdiction of the International Criminal Court to conduct the trial and pass judgment. He treats challenges to the validity of the alleged 'kidnapping' as 'a long shot' (the ICC has settled similar issues) and notes that raising every conceivable angle, including Duterte's frail medical condition, may merely delay his release if he is later acquitted on merits. Under the Rome Statute, the ICC's Office of the Chief Prosecutor (OCP) has two years from a state's withdrawal effective date to conduct preliminary examination and to apply in the three-member Pre-Trial Chamber (PTC) for authority to conduct an official investigation. PH withdrawal took effect March 17, 2019; then-Chief Prosecutor Fatou Bensouda petitioned the PTC on May 24, 2021 to investigate the 'situation' from Nov 1, 2011 to March 16, 2019; the PTC granted the petition on Sept 15, 2021 ├óŌé¼ŌĆØ both dates more than two years after the withdrawal effective date. The OCP argued that the two-year window was not breached because Bensouda actually began her preliminary examination on Feb 8, 2018, while the PH was still an ICC member. The PTC denied the PH petition to stop Karim Khan (Bensouda's successor) from continuing the investigation; the five-member Appeals Chamber, voting 3-2, denied the PH appeal ├óŌé¼ŌĆØ but only on subsidiarity grounds (PH not 'willing or able to genuinely carry out the investigation'). The majority refused to take up the two-year prescriptive issue because it had not been raised in the appeal. The minority of two judges (one already retired) argued that the ICC did not acquire jurisdiction because the two-year clock should start from the date the PTC granted authority (Sept 15, 2021), not from the date the OCP unilaterally began its own examination. Panganiban, citing his Dec 11, 2023 piece, respectfully agrees with the minority based on Philippine law: 'the OCP is not an integral part of the ICC. Therefore, the OCP's unilateral examination that began on Feb. 8, 2018, could not be counted as the start of the two-year prescriptive period. If this is not so, why is the OCP required to secure authority from the PTC to start the investigation?' He surfaces the strongest counter to his own view: in many European jurisdictions, prosecutors are deemed parts of the judicial system, so Bensouda's examination would satisfy the two-year limitation, and the prescriptive issue cannot save Duterte. He closes by warning that if the motion-to-dismiss is denied or deferred until after trial on merits, Duterte may be detained for many years before judgment ├óŌé¼ŌĆØ and asks, 'Given his advanced age and frail health, could he last that long?'

one_paragraph_summary: Published January 14, 2012 Ś days after 188 House members filed the verified impeachment complaint against CJ Renato C. Corona Ś this column performs CJP's constitutional-pedagogical reading of due process in impeachment. The Constitution provides two methods: the first, single-complainant method requires notice and hearing at the House Committee on Justice; the second, supermajority method (1/3 of all House members filing the verified complaint directly) constitutes the Articles of Impeachment outright and triggers Senate trial 'forthwith' Ś no House hearing required. The Bill of Rights due-process clause ('No person shall be deprived of life, liberty or property without due process of law') does NOT cover the return of a misused public office to the state because, per jurisprudence, no one has 'a vested right or a Torrens title to a public office'; 'public office is a public trust.' Due process, however, is sourced beyond the Constitution Ś from the natural-law tradition of Themistocles ('Strike, but hear me first') and Daniel Webster Ś and the Senate, which has 'sole power to try and decide all cases of impeachment,' provides the due-process satisfaction via its Senate Rules. Requiring notice and hearing where 1/3 of the House is already convinced would constitute 'over due process' and a 'moro-moro of false impartiality.' Impeachment is sui generis Ś a class of its own Ś not a judicial proceeding; the penalty is removal and possible disqualification, not life or property deprivation, so the constitutional framers correctly allowed ex-parte filing under the second method.

one_paragraph_summary: Published July 3, 2011 in two paired parts. The first critiques Gamboa vs Teves (June 28, 2011, penned by Justice Antonio T. Carpio) Ś which limited the 60-40 Filipino-capital rule to voting common shares only Ś on procedural-due-process grounds. Since 1935, the SEC has uniformly held that 'capital' includes both voting and non-voting shares; foreign investors relied on this and invested accordingly. Yet none of them were summoned, served, or heard before the SC ruling that would force them to unload their investments at bargain prices. Manuel V. Pangilinan was sued as managing director of First Pacific, but First Pacific itself was not impleaded, nor were the foreign chambers of commerce. CJP invokes Themistocles ('Strike, but hear me first!') and Daniel Webster ('a law that hears before it condemns, proceeds upon inquiry and renders judgment only after trial') and concludes 'with due respect' that 'no one, not even the highest court of the land' may impose such a restriction without hearing those most adversely affected Ś bracketing the merits to focus on procedure. The second part explains the Supreme Court's June 7, 2011 resolution implementing the long-delayed Memorandum of Agreement (April 6, 2011) between the DBM and the Philippine Judges Association. RA 9227 (effective November 11, 2006), fully implemented during CJP's tenure as Chief Justice, doubled magistrate compensation via the SAJ Ś necessary to fill 600 trial-court vacancies (~30%) and to discourage graft. But two subsequent rounds of pay increases (Arroyo's twin 10% increments and the 2009 Joint Resolution No. 4 tranches) were charged to the SAJ and made taxable, so 'instead of being increased, judicial take home pay was effectively decreased!' The new SC resolution restores tax-free treatment retroactive to June 24, 2010, but creates new problems: SAJ surpluses for rank-and-file vanish, and possible docket-fee increases would burden poor litigants. CJP's recommended fix: Congress should fund magistrate pay increases from national coffers, as it does for other government offices.

one_paragraph_summary: Published April 10, 2023 as a follow-up to CJP's previous Monday column on the US judicial-activism debate, this piece argues that the 1987 Philippine Constitution makes activism a DUTY rather than a philosophical choice. Article VIII Section 1 grants judicial power in two parts: ORDINARY review of actual controversies, and EXTRAORDINARY (expanded certiorari) review of grave abuse of discretion amounting to lack or excess of jurisdiction. By imposing the second as a DUTY ('not just a power or authority'), the Constitution has 'mandated [judges] to be activists' Ś and 'a duty cannot under any circumstance be evaded.' The judiciary cannot interfere with Congress's INTERNAL lawmaking rules or with confidential executive orders, but when the Constitution imposes 'conditions, limitations, or restrictions' (such as the one-year ban on repeat impeachment proceedings), the judiciary may enforce them. The paradigm is Francisco v. House of Representatives (November 10, 2003): the SC restrained the House from continuing the second impeachment complaint against CJ Hilario G. Davide Jr. Ś and 'after several days that tested its adherence to the rule of law,' the House 'reluctantly' obeyed. Philippine judges must additionally be libertarian: the Bill of Rights replicates US libertarian jurisprudence (Brown's 'separate is not equal,' Miranda's 'read him his rights,' one-man-one-vote, fruit of the poisonous tree) but anchors it on the 'impregnable Constitution' rather than changeable jurisprudence. 'American magistrates are accorded the luxury of choosing between being passivists or activists in their philosophical leaning. This luxury is denied to all Philippine judges.' The column closes with a personal tribute to recently-retired Judge Soliman M. Santos Jr. of RTC Naga City Ś a 'soulmate' fellow-student-activist whose forthcoming book is titled 'Judicial Activist' and for which CJP was asked to write the Foreword. CJP recalls his own student activism (youngest president of FEU Central Student Organization; co-founder/president of NUSP, 'the largest student organization in our country since then up to now') and gently regrets that Judge Santos did not reach the Supreme Court.

one_paragraph_summary: Published February 19, 2024, this column assesses the rule of law as 'besieged' in both the South China Sea and the East China Sea due to aggressive Chinese claims over Taiwan, ~80% of the SCS, and ECS islets (Diaoyu/Senkaku), and the challenges posed by riparian states and Western powers. CJP traces the historical arc: China's long history 'rivals Egypt and its pharaohs, and Greece and its Olympic gods,' followed by the 1850s-1950s decay producing 'shameful, one-sided treaties' that left a cultural conviction that 'losing face is worse than losing life.' WWII brought Japanese occupation of the Paracels and Spratlys; the 1945 Kuomintang acceptance of surrender per the Cairo and Potsdam declarations; the 1950 Mao takeover; Vietnam War realignment (North Vietnam relinquished claims to PRC, then post-1975 unified Vietnam adopted the South's sovereignty claims). Current occupation tally: ~29 features by Vietnam, 11 by the Philippines, 7 by China, 6 by Malaysia, 2 by Taiwan, 1 by Brunei. Despite being the second-largest economy with the biggest standing military, nuclear arsenal, and space program, China is constricted Ś only North Korea, Cambodia, and Mongolia are dependable neighbors Ś and needs the SCS for economic sea lanes, security first-line defense, and nuclear-submarine passage. The legal bases differ fundamentally: PRC claims rest on history/discovery/occupation/treaty cession across sea-air-land; the Philippine Arbitral Award (under UNCLOS) covers sovereign rights over the maritime domain ONLY. The AA, though unrecognized by China, outlawed the nine-dash (now ten-dash) line and declared freedom of navigation Ś which Western powers (US, Australia, France, UK) invoke despite not being parties. The Philippines is 'an international pygmy in the rule of force but a giant in the rule of law.' Duterte's placation failed; the Marcos pivot to US engagement, diplomatic protests, and open-media exposure of Chinese 'gray zone tactics' (water cannons, lane cuttings) puts China to 'international discomfiture.' Closing question: while China may have the right under the rule of law to recover its 'renegade province' Taiwan, will it also observe the rule of law in respecting Philippine maritime rights under the AA and UNCLOS?

one_paragraph_summary: Published February 12, 2024, this column surveys three jurisdictions where the rule of law is besieged: the Philippines (with its alleged inability or unwillingness to investigate war-on-drugs crimes against humanity), the United States (with ~100 cases against former President Donald Trump Ś 'the most SUIT-able presidential aspirant in US history'), and the International Court of Justice (with complicated genocide cases). CJP first clarifies a constitutive distinction: both the ICJ and ICC sit in The Hague, but the ICJ (UN judicial organ, born October 24, 1945, 193 members) handles STATE-vs-STATE litigations while the ICC (under the Rome Statute, in force July 1, 2002, 123 members) handles crimes against humanity against INDIVIDUALS Ś and the two most powerful countries (US, China) are ICJ members but NOT ICC members. The ICJ recently accepted jurisdiction over Russia's claim that Ukraine committed genocide against pro-Russian populations in Donetsk and Luhansk (Russia's stated justification for invasion); its March 2022 interim order for Moscow to halt was legally binding but unenforceable, though it emboldened Western allies to send arms, medicine, and food. On January 26, 2024, the ICJ ruled 15-2 that it has jurisdiction over South Africa's accusation that Israel is committing genocide against Palestinians in Gaza Ś declining to order a ceasefire but issuing six commands (equivalent to a preliminary mandatory injunction) covering prevention of civilian killing, military restraint, punishing incitement, ensuring basic services, preserving war-crime evidence, and submitting a compliance report by February 26. CJP awaits with bated breath whether Israel will comply, framing the question as a test of 'the faith of the free world in the rule of law.' On the US front, Scotus's oral arguments on February 8, 2024 saw Trump's lawyer 'incredibly argue' that the January 6, 2021 Capitol assault was 'a riot, not an insurrection' Ś and Scotus will also rule on the US Court of Appeals' rejection of Trump's presidential-immunity defense (which the CA correctly limited to INCUMBENT chief executives). The bigger question is timing: if the courts wait until after the November elections and Trump wins, presidential immunity of an INCUMBENT may moot all the cases. The column closes with a forward marker Ś '(The rule of law in the South and North China Sea in a future column.)' Ś explicitly setting up CA07.

one_paragraph_summary: Panganiban asks how the Philippines should enforce the July 12, 2016 Arbitral Award against China's intractable claim of ownership and sovereignty over almost the entire South China Sea including the Philippine exclusive economic zone. His foundational answer is to stick to the rule of law ├óŌé¼ŌĆØ drawing on his 1960s professorship in public international law, he teaches that poor and weak states like the Philippines can vie with wealthy and powerful countries only through 'the might of being right.' He walks through UN Charter Art. 2 par. 3 (peaceful settlement obligation) and Art. 33 par. 1 (the catalogue of modes ├óŌé¼ŌĆØ negotiation, enquiry, mediation, conciliation, arbitration, judicial settlement, regional agencies, other peaceful means), noting that the Philippines has tried all except ICJ judicial settlement. He distinguishes arbitration (ad hoc tribunal, ad hoc members, dissolves after award) from the ICJ (permanent body, judges with secure nine-year tenure, elected by UNGA and UNSC voting separately from a list nominated by national groups in the Permanent Court of Arbitration) ├óŌé¼ŌĆØ noting that all UN members are ipso facto ICJ members under the ICJ Statute, and disclosing that the Philippine National Group in the PCA (composed of Judge Raul Pangalangan, retired CJ Reynato Puno, retired Justice Jose Vitug, and the author as chair) nominated Judge Yuji Iwasawa of Japan, who won an ICJ seat on Nov 12, 2020 ├óŌé¼ŌĆØ with the late CJ Cesar Bengzon as the only Filipino who ever served as an ICJ judge in the late 1960s. He records his texted exchange with Justice Secretary Menardo I. Guevarra (who 'always comments on my columns') who asked whether ICJ resort might reopen the AA. Panganiban's reply: not if a good petition is crafted; the maritime rights already won under UNCLOS should not be re-litigated; China will likely take the opposite view because it does not recognize the AA; and 'how to get China to the ICJ is quite tough.' He notes that forcible modes (boycott, blockade, embargo, severance of diplomatic relations, armed encounters) are extremely difficult to win with, and endorses retired Justice Francis H. Jardeleza et al.'s proposal to identify by name and coordinates at least 100 features being claimed and occupied by the Philippines to help prove territorial title to 35 HTEs ├óŌé¼ŌĆØ though he expects China to ignore the proposal and insist on its old 'nine-dash line' tune. China has publicly declared that only the ICJ has jurisdiction over EEZ claims (because they involve territorial disputes) but will not consent ├óŌé¼ŌĆØ the ICJ Statute dispositive limitation. Given these difficulties, he proposes to Sec. Guevarra to form a team of international jurisconsults headed by Guevarra and Foreign Secretary Teodoro Locsin Jr. to study how to overcome these obstacles, promising to detail his humble suggestions in a future column.

one_paragraph_summary: Panganiban summarizes the July 12, 2016 UNCLOS Annex VII arbitral award in three parts. First, the tribunal rejected China's 'nine-dash line' and its claim of 'indisputable sovereignty' over almost the entire SCS, leaving the sea open to freedom of navigation. Second, it upheld PH maritime entitlements derived from archipelagic-state baselines ŌĆö 12-NM territorial sea, 24-NM contiguous zone, 200-NM EEZ, and 350-NM extended continental shelf ŌĆö collectively known as the West Philippine Sea, the sea area along the west side of the country from Aparri to Sulu. Third, it held that no disputed maritime feature qualifies as an 'island' with its own maritime rights, including Scarborough Shoal, which is merely a high-tide elevation generating only a 12-NM territorial sea around a protruding rock. He clarifies the scope: the arbitration covered only maritime rights, not land features, occupations, reclamations, or installations. The freedom-of-navigation portion is being de facto enforced by US, Australian, British, and Japanese forces because doing so serves their own permanent interests; the maritime-entitlements portions, however, are left to PH care because no other power's interests are independently engaged. Invoking Lord Palmerston ŌĆö 'in international relations there are no permanent friends or permanent enemies, only permanent interests' ŌĆö and pointing to 'America First,' he concludes that mutual defense pacts contain no express PH-maritime-rights obligations; the question of how the PH can enforce its own rights becomes the topic for the next column.

one_paragraph_summary: Reacting to Duterte's July 22, 2019 State of the Nation Address, Panganiban quotes the President's unambiguous declaration ŌĆö 'the West Philippine Sea is ours. There are no ifs and buts. It is ours' ŌĆö and estimates the speech devoted roughly 18% of its length (Ōēł17 of 93 minutes) to the WPS controversy. Duterte explained that conflict-avoidance and resource protection compel a 'delicate balancing act,' and that better results come from 'the privacy of a conference room than in a squabble in public,' paraphrasing Ecclesiastes 3 to defer aggressive enforcement to 'in due time.' Senior Justice Antonio T. Carpio reacted immediately, arguing that, factually, China does not possess the entire WPS, and that the President cannot by himself allow foreign nationals to fish in the EEZ because the Constitution (Art. XIII, Sec. 2, 2nd par.) reserves marine wealth exclusively to Filipinos. CJ fact-checks the fact-checker: Carpio is correct on the textual reading, but a liberal interpretation may permit the President ŌĆö with two-thirds Senate concurrence ŌĆö to enter into such a treaty. And because the Constitution makes any such agreement not 'valid and effective' without Senate concurrence, Filipinos need not fear a lopsided Xi-Duterte deal. By the SONA itself, CJ argues, Duterte has asserted ŌĆö not waived ŌĆö PH maritime rights and the arbitral award, preserving future enforcement options for his successors. He closes with a toast to the President's bold sovereignty incantation ŌĆö 'Cheers!'

one_paragraph_summary: Published April 25, 2021 amid Chinese swarming of the West Philippine Sea with 200+ militia and coast guard ships and the prohibition of Filipino fishing in the EEZ, this column organizes itself around the binary 'Might is right' (rule of force) versus 'Right is might' (rule of law). CJP traces the historical irony: Great Britain forced 'Might is right' on China via the shameful Opium Wars and the cruel Treaty of Nanking and other 'Unequal Treaties' in the mid-1800s Ś and CJP had hoped the new China, with its hi-tech military and economic dominance, would have learned the lesson and embraced 'Right is might' instead. Instead, China 'has, in fact, replaced Great Britain as the new bully in Asia.' The July 12, 2016 501-page Arbitral Award expressly recognized Philippine exclusive rights to fish, exploit, and develop the natural resources of the WPS within the 200 NM/370 km EEZ under UNCLOS Ś and China, a UNCLOS signatory, is legally bound to obey. Crucially, the AA covers SOVEREIGN RIGHTS over the maritime domain ONLY, NOT SOVEREIGNTY over land features (which the Philippines did not ask for and the AA did not grant). Asymmetrically, China can swarm Philippine fishermen but cannot 'swarm and shoo away the navies and air forces of the United States, Australia, France, and Great Britain' Ś which conduct freedom of navigation in the WPS while invoking parts of the AA, though none of them were parties to the arbitral proceedings and the US 'is not even a signatory to UNCLOS.' CJP wishes the Western powers 'would enforce the entire AA to show their consistent adherence to Right is might.' China's response has been to refuse to recognize the AA and to insist that only the ICJ has jurisdiction (since the conflict allegedly involves land sovereignty); Justice Antonio T. Carpio (April 15, 2021) and Prof. Jay Batongbacal argue, Au contraire, that Philippine sovereignty over Spratlys and Scarborough is provable via the 1734 Murillo-Velarde map and the chain of cessions (1898 Treaty of Paris, Spain to US; 1900 Treaty of Washington, US to Philippines). If the Philippines has a strong case, the government can accept China's ICJ challenge Ś under the ICJ Charter, only states, not private parties, may file. Closing rhetorical question: 'Might is right vs Right is might. Which will prevail?'

He recaps China's May 7, 2009 submission of the nine-dash line ŌĆö which appropriated roughly 80% of the PH EEZ ŌĆö and its aggressive seizure-and-build strategy in the Spratlys, then walks through China's jurisdictional objection to the Aquino-initiated UNCLOS Annex VII arbitration (China insisted the proper forum was the ICJ, which would have required Chinese consent). Let the great powers tangle over freedom of navigation and the nine-dash line; the PH should focus on its God-given wealth in the EEZ. He closes by asking Filipinos to unite as one people and pray for the President as he 'tiptoes to his talks' with Xi.

Ramos opened to wide presidential discretion). one_paragraph_summary: Triggered by the Myanmar coup, the AFP's withdrawal from the UP-DND accord, and Duterte's growing reliance on retired military officers, Panganiban traces the constitutional architecture of civilian supremacy. Art. II, Sec. 3 ŌĆö together with eight other provisions detailing the Commander-in-Chief role, martial-law limits, Commission on Appointments confirmation, Ombudsman jurisdiction, oath, professionalism, the partisan-activity ban, and the bar on civilian positions ŌĆö establishes the principle. Unlike the restive militaries in Myanmar and Thailand, the AFP has been faithful: it defended Cory Aquino from eight putsch attempts, helped topple Estrada when People Power 2 had popular support, and (per Avelino Cruz Jr. and Albert del Rosario) blocked revolutionary-government attempts. There is no imminent risk of a takeover. What CJ cannot understand is the AFP's hostility to constitutionally guaranteed academic freedom. Drawing on his own poverty ŌĆö peddling newspapers, shining shoes, selling textbooks at FEU ŌĆö he confesses that 'Karl Marx was truly enticing' to the poor, but Fr. Michael Nolan and Dean Alejandro R. Roces at FEU taught him 'the superiority of democracy and the value of education to rise from poverty,' which is why he co-founded the National Union of Students with the UP, UST, San Beda, and St. Theresa's councils to resist red infiltration in the 1950s, when 'the rebels were almost at the gates of Manila' and Magsaysay galvanized the AFP with the promise of 'freedom and food, justice and jobs, liberty and prosperity.' Breaking the UP-DND accord, he warns, would alienate the educated poor from the State the AFP vowed to protect ŌĆö 'unreasonable restrictions on freedom favor the enemies of the rule of law, and the quickest way to lose the red sands of youth is to squeeze the hand too tightly.'

one_paragraph_summary: Published February 2, 2018, this column dissects the difference between RULE OF LAW (laws as social contracts protecting human rights and freedom) and RULE BY LAW (laws as instruments imposed by rulers to enforce their will). CJP traces the cultural origins of Filipino lawlessness Ś a feudal society that encourages impunity (the powerful flaunt the law and get away; the rest treat law as a game) Ś and diagnoses the linguistic inheritance: Tagalog 'batas' actually means 'to decree,' something imposed by rulers; English 'law' from old Norse means 'that which is set, or laid down.' Modern rule of law as social contract developed only in the last three centuries alongside human rights and freedom. The World Justice Project's Rule of Law Index Ś annual since 2010, with 9 factors broken into 44 subfactors, four universal principles (accountability, just laws, open government, accessible and impartial dispute resolution), and a general-population poll of 1,000 respondents in Manila, Cebu, and Davao Ś shows the Philippines dropping dramatically: 51st of 102 (2015) ? 70th of 113 (2016) ? 88th of 113 in the latest report. 'Our drop of 18 places was the worst in the world.' Comparative analysis disproves the simple authoritarianism-causes-good-governance thesis: China ranks 75th and Singapore 13th, but the lowest-ranked countries (Venezuela and Cambodia) also have one-party rule. What matters is whether checks and balances function. The Marcos martial law era is the Philippine cautionary tale of rule BY law leading to economic ruin. The totalitarian temptation grows in crisis. The January 21, 2018 Davao police incident Ś three minors arrested, mauled, made to kneel apologizing to President Duterte's photograph Ś shows the dismantling of rule of law moving 'from an almost playful cat-and-mouse routine to an acceptance of unlawful arrest, detention and execution.' Mass transit disasters and the vaccine scandal are concrete cases of squandered public funds and endangered lives. The column closes with stakes-and-winners-losers questions about ongoing Charter-change debates: will rule of law be strengthened, or will rule BY law Ś particularly by local warlords Ś gain ground?

one_paragraph_summary: Published August 7, 2023, this column analyzes the July 18, 2023 ICC Appeals Chamber 3-2 ruling authorizing Chief Prosecutor Karim Khan ('a celebrated Queen's Counsel in the United Kingdom') to resume his investigation of the alleged 'crime against humanity of murder' in the Philippine 'war on drugs' (November 1, 2011 - March 16, 2019). CJP organizes around the temporal-vs-strategic distinction: Khan's victory is TEMPORAL only, while the Philippine government Ś though defeated procedurally Ś won STRATEGIC knowledge. He walks through the chronology with deliberate emphasis ('Please note the dates carefully'): Bensouda's February 8, 2018 preliminary examination announcement; Duterte's March 17, 2018 ICC withdrawal; the March 17, 2019 effective withdrawal; Bensouda's May 24, 2021 PTC request for formal preliminary investigation authority; the PTC's September 15, 2021 grant; Khan's suspension at Philippine request for domestic inquiry; the PTC's January 26, 2023 resumption grant after deeming the PH investigation insufficient; and the AC's July 18, 2023 ruling. The two main appeal issues were COMPLEMENTARITY (under Rome Statute Article 17) and JURISDICTION. On complementarity, the AC sided with Khan: the government had not shown genuine willingness and ability to investigate. On jurisdiction, the AC majority of three skirted the issue procedurally, holding that the Philippines should have appealed the September 15, 2021 decision (which it failed to do) rather than the January 26, 2023 decision Ś thus the Philippines had waived its right to challenge jurisdiction. The minority of two insisted the majority erred: should arrest warrants be issued, the issue will inevitably resurface, so proceeding without ruling on jurisdiction wastes time and resources. The minority's substantive position: the ICC did not acquire jurisdiction because both Bensouda's May 24, 2021 request and the PTC's September 15, 2021 grant fell MORE THAN TWO YEARS AFTER the Philippine withdrawal took effect on March 17, 2019. CJP predicts Khan may abandon the case to pursue 'situations like Russia's unprovoked war on Ukraine.' The Philippine government, even in defeat, gained strategic knowledge: two of five judges publicly support dismissal on jurisdiction; none of the three majority judges took an opposing position. The column closes with CJP's own doctrinal answer to the question on which the case turns: 'Is the preliminary examination of Bensouda in 2018 an integral part of the preliminary investigation authorized by the ICC?' His short answer: 'No.'

one_paragraph_summary: Panganiban analyzes ICC chief prosecutor Karim Khan's request to resume preliminary investigation into alleged crimes against humanity committed during Rodrigo Duterte's incumbency as Davao City mayor (Nov 1, 2011-June 30, 2016) and as president (July 1, 2016-March 16, 2019), declining to engage the political overtones and instead surfacing three crucial legal issues. On sovereignty, he argues that the Philippines partly surrendered its absolute control when the Senate ratified the Rome Statute on Aug 30, 2011, and is bound to the Statute's continuing-jurisdiction provision for crimes committed during membership. On the kind of crimes, he sets out Article 5 (genocide, crimes against humanity, war crimes, aggression) and Article 7's definition of crimes against humanity as acts 'committed as part of a widespread and systematic attack directed against any civilian population' ŌĆö refusing to rule on the factual question raised by OSG (Menardo Guevarra) that the killings were 'legitimate operations against illicit drugs,' not state policy. On complementarity (Article 17), the pivotal issue, he argues that DOJ and OSG must demonstrate willingness, ability, and genuineness in prosecuting the lawless killers of drug suspects (6,117 official, 30,000 critics) to defeat ICC jurisdiction. He warns that if arrest warrants issue against Duterte and his officials, the Philippines' reputation will be severely tarnished and 'numbered among failed African states,' with the Supreme Court ŌĆö admired by foreign chief justices he hosted during his tenure ŌĆö unfairly smeared by association.

Cayetano, March 16, 2021, J Leonen, backed this via obiter); or (3) cooperate without rejoining ŌĆö opening NBI, PNP, and DOJ Chief Prosecutor facilities under comity of nations, an option that could end either in ICC concluding the Philippines is genuinely willing and able, or in ICC investigators ensnaring perpetrators 'into the sticky ICC web.' VP Sara Duterte has formally asked the DOJ not to cooperate, favoring the status quo. He closes with the Tagalog cliffhanger: 'Abangan.'

Had the three new appointees voted with their conservative seniors, the result would have been 5-4 for Trump. Trump's 'last card' at the January 6 congressional electoral count is 'really a joker' because Senate Republican leader Mitch McConnell has already conceded, acknowledging that 'our system of government has processes to determine who will be sworn in on January the 20th.' For CJ, the SCOTUS rebuff shows that 'despite Trump's occasional megalomania and authoritarian streak, the rule of law is much alive in the US' and that America, 'after losing its way during the last four years... could now be looked up to by the free world for leadership.' He draws the cross-jurisdictional parallel to Estrada v. Desierto (March 2, 2001), in which the Philippine SC unanimously (13-0, with CJ Davide and CJ Panganiban inhibiting) ousted Estrada in favor of VP Arroyo. Six years after his conviction-and-pardon, Estrada sought CJ out at Jose T. Pardo's Ayala Alabang house and asked why none of his own SC appointees dissented and 'If they could not side with me, why did they not just inhibit like you?' CJ's answer ├óŌé¼ŌĆØ preserved as the column's doctrinal core ├óŌé¼ŌĆØ is that judicial decisions 'must conform to the Constitution and the law, not to the wiles and whims of the governors,' and that those who 'don the Court's black robes are expected to undergo a transformation, to become different persons, to forfeit their past activities, relatives, and friends, especially the president who appointed them.' The moral lesson: 'presidents should assume a cutting of umbilical ties when jurists enter the hallowed doors of the Supreme Court. In fact, I believe, they should be happy, or at least consoled, that their appointees lived up to their sworn duty of Fiat justitia ruat caelum' ├óŌé¼ŌĆØ Let justice be done though the heavens may fall.

one_paragraph_summary: Panganiban celebrates the Supreme Court's unanimous ruling in Deduro v. Vinoya (July 4, 2023, released May 8, 2024) ŌĆö a 39-page treatise expertly penned by Justice Rodil V. Zalameda ŌĆö issuing the writ of amparo to a Red-tagged petitioner, calling it 'a victory of activists' from journalists and students to laborers, jeepney drivers, and plain citizens exercising freedom of expression via rallies, writings, speeches, and even body language like fist clenching, sneering, and staring. He carefully distinguishes the 'writ of amparo' (ex parte issuance on prima facie face-of-petition showing) from the 'privilege of the writ of amparo' (granted only after summary hearings on substantial evidence), analogizing to but distinguishing from preliminary vs permanent injunction in civil cases. He bestows on retired Justice Adolfo S. Azcuna the title 'Father of the Writ of Amparo in the Philippines' ŌĆö Azcuna, as 1991 bar examiner, asked the question very few answered correctly, having earlier proposed inclusion of the writ in the 1973 Constitution (failed) and the 1986 Bill of Rights (redirected to SC powers by CJ Roberto Concepcion). He explains the writ's coverage of (a) extralegal killings, (b) enforced disappearances (per RA 10353), and (c) threats thereof ŌĆö including, per SND v. Manalo (Oct 7, 2008), threats to security existing independently of deprivation of liberty ŌĆö making Red-tagging a cognizable cause of action because the Red-tagged person becomes a target of vigilantes, paramilitary groups, or state agents. Deduro must still produce substantial evidence at the summary proceedings to obtain the actual privilege. CJ defers parties and process to law students because the treatise 'could erupt in another bar exam.'

one_paragraph_summary: Panganiban opens with the Bill of Rights verbatim ├óŌé¼ŌĆØ no person shall be deprived of life, liberty or property without due process of law, nor shall any person be denied the equal protection of the laws ├óŌé¼ŌĆØ and explains that these twin rights safeguard especially the poor and powerless from government tyranny. He distinguishes substantive (objectives and intrinsic validity of laws) from procedural (means and methods) due process and lays out the five-element legal definition: impartial court, jurisdiction over subject matter, jurisdiction over person, opportunity to be heard, judgment only after hearing. He uses live PDAF examples ├óŌé¼ŌĆØ Jinggoy Estrada's due-process petition over the OOO's initial document-furnishing denial (mooted when Ombudsman Morales promptly sent the documents) and routine challenges to Sandiganbayan jurisdiction ├óŌé¼ŌĆØ to ground the framework. On equal protection he explains that equality is not absolute because factual situations differ and need classification (aliens vs citizens, senior-citizen 20-percent discount, the Estrada plunder Special Division still assailed by Saguisag, the Ampatuan-Solis-Reyes docket-clearance now criticized by defense lawyers). He rejects the reader-proposed Singapore/Malaysia authoritarian shortcut ├óŌé¼ŌĆØ Filipinos tried martial law and it failed in our milieu, causing more injustices, and will not exchange hard-won freedoms for harsh efficiencies of strongman rule. His prescription is an innovative and courageous judiciary backed by vigilant citizenry, redefining the twin rights case-by-case because the Charter left them undefined, with the Court revisiting age-old rights to keep them in step with digital and biotechnological revolutions. He closes with the titular rallying cry: jurisprudence must be stable but it cannot stand still, the Supreme Court has the will and the weapons to simplify legal processes and allow good governance and sterling economic growth to endure ├óŌé¼ŌĆØ Yes, the Supreme Court can.

one_paragraph_summary: At last week's FLP awards ceremony for 20 new law scholars, five MBA fellows, and 30 dissertation contest winners ŌĆö with First Lady Louise Araneta Marcos, herself 'a brilliant lawyer,' as special guest ŌĆö keynote speaker Manuel V. Pangilinan (MVP) reassured that lawyers and the public should not fear AI's disruptions 'except that it will drastically reduce their billable hours.' MVP defined generative AI as the capacity to curate huge data and create new content (video, audio, text) resembling human intelligence, with loopback learning making it more human-like over time ŌĆö yet reassured that 'you will not be replaced by AI per se, but you can be replaced by a person using AI.' Algorithms are AI's 'homecourt' but 'can be rigid and fundamentalist'; humans must distinguish themselves not by competing with algorithms but by exercising 'liberty of thought, our talent to think, and combining our creative and innovative powers with AI' in the continuing pursuit of prosperity ŌĆö robots cannot replace human touch, empathy, instincts, ability to connect, or capacity to navigate crisis emotions. Rachel Lois B. Gella's first-prize 77-page dissertation at USLS Bacolod cited CJ's prosperity motto and argued that 'consent permeates our lives in the digital landscape,' that commercial practices can render that consent illusory, and ŌĆö paraphrasing Justice George Malcolm ŌĆö that 'liberty cannot be dwarfed into mere freedom from physical restraint' and 'there will be no real liberty in the digital world when consent is rendered illusory.' Though of older vintage than AI, the 1987 Constitution's guarantee that 'the privacy of communication and correspondence shall be inviolable,' together with the ICCPR, the Civil Code, and the Data Privacy Act of 2012, can protect digital consent. The SC's Strategic Plan for Judicial Innovation 2022-2027 anticipates the AI age, with its data-sharing alliance with DOJ and DILG cutting down redundancies and delays. Congress is to be hailed for HB 7396 (Artificial Intelligence Authority) and HB 7913 (AI system audit and accountability). 'Properly contextualized and used, AI can fortify the safeguarding of liberty and the nurturing of prosperity under the rule of law.'

one_paragraph_summary: Panganiban answers his own rhetorical question from the prior Monday's column ŌĆö why China militarizes the South China Sea despite being a UNCLOS signatory and 2016 Arbitral Award party-litigant, and why the US (a non-signatory and non-party-litigant) enforces SCS freedom of navigation with Australia, France, Germany, and Great Britain ŌĆö with the unsaid short and simple answer: 'national interest.' He argues that national interest is not constant but shifts with political, economic, security, and social aspirations and turmoil. China's SCS posture is locked in by decades of school and media teaching that the SCS belongs to China, reinforced by civilizational pride and grievance over Western enslavement and the long-term leases of Hong Kong and Macau, and propelled by China's meteoric rise from poverty to the second-largest economy seeking parity with or supremacy over the West. The US has itself shifted ŌĆö from interwar neutrality (covertly aiding European powers from Sept 1, 1939) to full engagement after the Dec 7, 1941 Pearl Harbor attack, then to post-1945 Pax Americana exporting American-style democracy, libertarianism, capitalism, and free trade at the cost of trillions ('ironically' much borrowed from China) and American boots in Korea, Vietnam, Afghanistan, and Iraq ŌĆö and again on Nov 5, 2024, with Trump's 'Make America Great Again' mandate to demand allied burden-sharing, deport illegal migrants, and disrupt the postwar order ('Verily, he loves being a disruptor'). Philippine national interest has see-sawed similarly: tired of democratic compromise and dynasties/'imperial Manila,' Filipinos welcomed Duterte's autocratic 2016 pivot to China and away from US protection; in 2022 Marcos Jr. reverted to the US, gaining the first absolute majority (over 50%) under the 1987 Constitution ŌĆö a mandate CJ argues licenses him to lead in protecting and projecting national interest vis-├Ā-vis ASEAN, China, the US, and the world.

one_paragraph_summary: Panganiban introduces the unique character of the Foundation for Liberty and Prosperity (FLP) law scholarships ŌĆö fully funded by the Tan Yan Kee Foundation at P200,000 per scholar per year (tuition, books, monthly stipends) ŌĆö which are granted on pure merit rather than financial need, with paucity of funds considered only as a tiebreaker among equally excellent applicants. The scholarship requires recipients to espouse, promote, and fulfill the liberty-and-prosperity philosophy: that legal education has overemphasized political freedoms (due process, equal protection, free speech, free assembly, presumption of innocence) and underemphasized economic and social freedoms (freedom from hunger and want, the right to pursue entrepreneurship and innovation, the right to conquer poverty through the rule of law) plus the corollary responsibility to share prosperity and equitably distribute the fruits of labor and capital. He asserts that 'freedom and food, justice and jobs, ethics and economics, nay, liberty and prosperity are equally important; one is useless without the other,' and that 'the best way to conquer poverty, to create wealth and to share prosperity is to unleash the entrepreneurial genius of people.' He provides the complete alphabetical roster of twenty current scholars (ten third-year and ten fourth-year, drawn from U of Cordilleras, FEU, San Beda, Ateneo de Manila, U of San Carlos, UST, U of Cebu, PUP, and De La Salle), the fifteen past FLP scholars taking the May 2018 bar (results expected May 3), the cash prize structure for bar topnotchers (P200,000 for first place, P100,000 each for second through tenth), and the full TYKF board (Dr. Lucio C. Tan as chair plus 14 trustees including Joaquin Bernas, Amando Tetangco Jr., and Cesar Virata) and FLP board (CJ as chair plus 8 trustees including his late wife Leni).

one_paragraph_summary: Panganiban marks the Foundation for Liberty and Prosperity's 10th anniversary by reflecting on the FLP's four October themes ├óŌé¼ŌĆØ theory versus practice, dreams versus realities, negative versus positive, slogans versus philosophies. He recalls his pre-law political science professor challenging him to refute the Marxist slogan 'From each according to his ability and to each according to his need,' a slogan 'difficult to refute' for a 'dirt-poor student' like him; yet he knew that communism, however appealing in theory, was appalling in practice, leading him later to forge his legal philosophy of 'safeguarding liberty and nurturing prosperity under the rule of law.' He observes that the Bill of Rights is written mainly in negative language ('No person shall be deprived of life, liberty or property without due process of law├óŌé¼┬”'), as are most of the Ten Commandments ('Thou shall not kill,' 'Thou shall not steal') and electoral mantras (Marcos's 'Alis diyan!' against Macapagal; Cory's 'Tama na! Sobra na! Palitan na!' against Marcos). Constitutional positives ├óŌé¼ŌĆØ like 'The goals of the national economy are a more equitable distribution of opportunities, income, and wealth' ├óŌé¼ŌĆØ and Jesus's two positive commands ('Love God with all your heart├óŌé¼┬” and love your neighbor as I love you') exist but are outnumbered, and slogans rarely transform into fulfillment without 'sustainable philosophies, implementable programs, and realistic timelines.' He confesses that even his SC decisions were mostly negative in language ├óŌé¼ŌĆØ commanding stops, dismissing petitions, enforcing negatively-phrased rights ├óŌé¼ŌĆØ with only a few on positive provisions, leaving a yearning that he had not done enough. After retirement, invitations from tycoons to serve as independent director or adviser revealed their own yearnings for philanthropy and CSR, and together with dear friends he organized the FLP on October 13, 2011. Its first decade (2011-2021) achieved sustainability, purchased an office condo HQ in Makati, and sponsored four projects (professorial chairs, legal scholarships, dissertation-writing contests, educational assistance for the poor), funded by over P164 million from family, partners, and friends ├óŌé¼ŌĆØ transforming 'the safeguarding of liberty├óŌé¼┬” from a mere dream to a sustainable reality' that will continue 'beyond my lifetime,' with the planned Interactive Museum of Liberty and Prosperity (in partnership with the Supreme Court) as the ultimate first-decade project. During its second decade (2021-2031), the FLP will enlarge its focus to help eliminate extreme poverty, initially by sponsoring scholarships and dissertation contests among MBA students in economics, sustainability, business law, and entrepreneurship ├óŌé¼ŌĆØ pivoting from the liberty side to the prosperity side of the overarching philosophy.

one_paragraph_summary: Panganiban summarizes his speech at the launch of 'Momentum,' an economic-reforms book by Romeo Bernardo, Calixto Chikiamco, Emmanuel de Dios, Raul Fabella, and the late Cayetano Paderanga. He proposes that the authors would constitute a 'Supreme Court for economic matters' and uses the launch to restate his core philosophy: 'Humans need both justice and jobs; freedom and food; ethics and economics; peace and development; liberty and prosperity' ├óŌé¼ŌĆØ twin beacons that must always go together because 'one is useless without the other,' achieved by unleashing the entrepreneurial genius of people. He cites Ta├ā┬▒ada v. Angara (May 2, 1997), which he authored ├óŌé¼ŌĆØ affirming Philippine WTO membership and adopting globalization, liberalization, deregulation, and privatization against the then-reigning 'Filipino First' doctrine ├óŌé¼ŌĆØ and recalls that he began with only three concurrences but persuaded the other 14 to a unanimous outcome. He recounts CJ Beverley McLachlin of Canada renaming the philosophy 'liberty and prosperity under the rule of law' to stress that 'egalitarian goal should be achieved only via libertarian methods,' and the Global Forum's Manila Declaration. He notes that after his retirement the Court reverted to giving primacy to political liberties ├óŌé¼ŌĆØ traceable to the Constitution's anti-colonial origins, where the Bill of Rights (borrowed from the US Constitution, epitomized by Patrick Henry's 'Give me liberty or give me death') is mandatory and self-executing while prosperity provisions are mostly aspirational. He founded the FLP in 2011 to give equal primacy, sponsoring three projects: 20 Metrobank Foundation professorial chairs on liberty and prosperity, 20 annual Tan Yan Kee Foundation P200,000 scholarships (first batch all passed the bar; two placed first and fifth, with CJ Peralta chairing the board), and Ayala Corporation├óŌé¼ŌĆ£funded dissertation contests (~P2 million in prizes, with Senior Justice Perlas-Bernabe as board chair). FLP plans a roving museum in 2020 and a Global Forum reconvening in 2021.

one_paragraph_summary: Panganiban opens the first part of his speech at the Asean Law Association's 12th general assembly opening luncheon (February 26, 2015, at Makati Shangri-La) with a famous quotation from Dr. Martin Luther King Jr.: 'If a man does not have a job or an income, he has neither life nor liberty nor the possibility for the pursuit of happiness.' It may seem ironic to begin a speech before the judicial and legal greats of Asean by citing an American civil rights icon ŌĆö a foreigner in the 10 member countries ŌĆö but CJ quotes MLK not because of his nationality, color, gender, or religion, but because of the truth he said so simply and profoundly: truth is eternal and limitless, not bound by sovereignty, territory, ideology, or legality. And that truth is this ŌĆö humans need both justice and jobs; freedom and food; ethics and economics; peace and development; liberty and prosperity; these twin beacons must always go together; one is useless without the other. CJ has always espoused 'Liberty and Prosperity' as the twin anchors of his being as a lawyer and as a jurist. Many of those present attended his valedictory activity as chief justice when on October 18-20, 2006 he convened a 'Global Forum on Liberty and Prosperity' at the same Makati Shangri-La. Even in retirement he continues the advocacy: in 2011, when he celebrated his 75th birthday, he organized the Foundation for Liberty and Prosperity, which is now honored to cosponsor the general assembly together with the Philippine Supreme Court and the Philippine ALA Chapter. Certain truths transcend sovereignties, territories, ideologies, and legalities. And one of these truths is this: the best way to conquer poverty, to create wealth, and to share prosperity is to unleash the entrepreneurial genius of people by granting them the freedom and the tools to help themselves and society. To prove the thesis, CJ quotes a popular adage from Confucius: 'Give a man a fish and you feed him for a day. Teach him to fish and you feed him for a lifetime.' Indeed, to save a fisherman from destitution, we must help him learn how to fish more effectively, educate him in the skills needed to catch fish more efficiently, assist him in acquiring a boat, allow him the freedom to sail the vast oceans, and teach him how to market his catch. Sometimes, some of us fear that the fisherman may get lost and die in storms, become selfish and want to own the entire ocean and its resources, or become too rich and powerful and metamorphose into a rival, an enemy, or worse, a master. Such fears of possible misjudgments may indeed happen some of the time ŌĆö human arrogance, greed, and avarice lurk in all undertakings, but they are the exceptions rather than the rule. We must never stop dreaming for fear that reality may shatter our dreams; that is part of the interesting reality of being human. On the other hand, the goal of governance and of law is to provide guarantees and incentives to help the fisherman prosper, to create the institutions to support him, and to promulgate minimal regulations to prevent him from appropriating all the fishing grounds, from keeping all the earnings to himself, and from forgetting his obligation to pay reasonable taxes ŌĆö government must also inspire him to share his consequential wealth with the rest of society. To validate this simple truth, take three countries. The United States, the most powerful country in the world and the great promoter of liberal democracy, attained affluence because of the pioneers who defied monarchical tyrannies and started a new nation that unleashed the inventive, innovative, and entrepreneurial spirit of people like Thomas Edison, Nikola Tesla, Cornelius Vanderbilt, John Rockefeller, Andrew Carnegie, Henry Ford, and J.P. Morgan ŌĆö and of great government leaders like Franklin Delano Roosevelt and Dwight Eisenhower who provided them with the encouragement to attain their dreams and the good governance to contain their greed and share their wealth. Then to China, the second most powerful economy in the world and the prime promoter of the communist system: true, Mao Zedong led the masses in a revolt that dislodged the corrupt and inefficient government born of an outdated monarchy, but it was Deng Xiaoping who led this nation to unparalleled economic prosperity by unleashing the entrepreneurial ingenuity of the Chinese under his 'One-Country-Two-Systems' philosophy. Finally, to Korea: as a result of World War II, this country was divided into North and South, which unfortunately could not accept their division and engaged in a terrible war that ruined their economies; rising from the ruins, South Korea relied on the entrepreneurial spirit of the Koreans, built on their private initiative, creativity, freedom, and hard work, and thereby ushered prosperity. In contrast, North Korea ŌĆö despite its technological and military bravado ŌĆö wallows in abject poverty as a result of its tight grip on creativity and on its inordinate fear of the entrepreneurship, education, and freedom of its people. one_paragraph_summary: Panganiban delivers a eulogy at the wake of Jovito R. Salonga, his guru and surrogate father. The 1956-57 school year FEU law students' strike protested the 'unjustified retirement' of their old dean ŌĆö and a 'youngish 36-year-old, bespectacled professor walked to center stage, took the microphone, and single-handedly faced the hostile crowd. The new dean, Dr. Salonga, spoke with a forceful tremolo and held the rambunctious crowd spellbound. In 30 minutes flat, the strikers trooped back to their classrooms, convinced of the error in their mass action, and of the wisdom, honesty and sincerity of their new dean.' Active as FEU Central Student Organization president and National Union of Students founder/president, CJ gave secondary importance to academics ŌĆö but Salonga's constant recitation-testing during peak student-activity periods produced 'most outstanding student' in third year and Latin honors graduation. Before the 1960 bar exams CJ got sick and faced a flooding rainstorm ŌĆö wading 'in knee-deep water from the then FEU HospitalŌĆ” to the University of the East, the exam venue' ŌĆö and after a lackluster first day wanted to quit; but Salonga 'would not hear of it,' and CJ ended up 'copping the sixth highest place.' On Salonga's recommendation, CJ won a Yale Law School scholarship but lacked funds to fly ŌĆö the American Embassy denied a travel grant, 'perhaps because of my student activism.' Salonga consoled him: 'Come, join my law office and I will teach you what I learned from the University of the Philippines, Yale and Harvard.' Thus began the surrogate-father tutorship ŌĆö CJ's biological father having passed during first-year prelaw ŌĆö and Salonga taught the rudiments of law practice and 'the life-defining values of integrity, prudence and fairness. He led by example, not by words. He was a dedicated husband, a caring father, a faithful friend and a devoted man of God. He did not smoke, drink or gamble. Wealth, power, worldly pleasures, titles and honors did not fascinate him.' The religious-faith difference (Salonga devout Protestant, CJ fledgling Catholic) 'did not diminish our over 50 years of surrogate father-son relationship, or my awe, esteem and affection for him. This is probably because we never discussed what separated us, only what truly bound us.' After three years as Salonga's law-office assistant, CJ asked permission to form his own law firm, receiving the blessing 'You now have formidable wings, go and soar the limitless sky!' The greatest honor in CJ's legal career came when Salonga, as Senate president and LP chief, retained CJ as his legal counsel ŌĆö 'not by awarding me a plaque of gold or conferring on me an honorary degree, but by retaining me as his legal counselŌĆ” Nothing is more fulfilling, more exhilarating, than the honor of being counsel to one's guru and former boss!' Before joining Cory Aquino's 1986 Cabinet, Salonga divested conflicting financial interests and dissolved his prestigious law firm 'not because this was mandated by law but because of his strict personal ethic of being beyond suspicion in serving government' ŌĆö and CJ followed his example by dissolving their law firm when joining the Supreme Court. Through multiple JBC screening failures preceding the SC appointment, Salonga 'constantly cheered me up and tirelessly lifted my sagging spirit,' patiently reminding him of Romans 8:28: 'God makes all things work together for the good of those who love Him and are called according to His purpose.' Upon CJ's retirement as Chief Justice, Salonga and Evelyn Miranda-Feliciano published 'A Test of Courage' (late 2007) as a tribute to the Panganiban Court. 'Those who have read my decisions and writings will surely detect the legal philosophy and life values of Dr. Salonga. Indeed, I try to echo, even if unworthily and faintly, his teaching that law cannot be separated from life, that it should be used as a brick in building the social edifice and as a means to fulfill the deepest aspirations of man. The mortal body of Dr. Salonga may have perished but his legacy and memory will live forever. Alleluia!'

Daughter Celine Panganiban-Hannett remembers Leni as 'my image of perfection ŌĆö perfection that was beyond my grasp,' raising the five children 'with quiet authority,' reasoning rather than yelling because 'eight was the age of reason,' and modeling lessons indirectly ŌĆö most famously through the monthly-allowance arrangement that taught Celine and Ate Len about banking and borrowing while teaching Archie about saving (though Mom still bought him pajamas anyway). Grandson Rafa Panganiban Yaptangco remembers Lola Leni as 'my Number One supporter,' greeting him with 'How are you? How's school? Do you want chicheria? Just get, just get, we have plenty!' ŌĆö her love for the newest phones (and Bounce) becoming the foundation of his tech career today. AIM classmate Mari Sison Garcia remembers their pioneering 1971 batch of four women among the men, Leni's 'difficult balance between the demands of the Harvard's case study method and being mother to her five children and a good wife to CJ Art,' their being 'First Fruits of AIM,' and later reunion as professors with lunches and behind-the-scene guidance ŌĆö and finally as Council of Servant Leaders in the Bukas Loob Sa Diyos Catholic Charismatic Community, where 'CJ Art changed the dynamics of worship and Leni remained his anchor.'

one_paragraph_summary: Panganiban addresses restive congressmen dismayed by alleged Supreme Court 'flip-flopping' ├óŌé¼ŌĆØ especially Belgica v. Ochoa (Nov 19 2013) outlawing PDAF and Reyes v. Comelec (June 25 2013) upholding the Comelec's unseating of Regina Ongsiako Reyes ├óŌé¼ŌĆØ who are threatening to disobey and impeach justices. He defends both decisions. On PDAF, LAMP v. DBM (April 24 2012) was reversed by Belgica a year later, but this is self-correction, not stare-decisis breach. On Reyes, he rewinds the four-stage chronology: March 27 Comelec First Division cancelled Reyes's COC (she lost Filipino citizenship by marriage to an American and failed to take oath of allegiance and renounce US citizenship); May 14 the en banc affirmed 6-1; May 18 the BOC proclaimed her winner despite the cancellation; May 19 the Comelec ruling became final and executory; June 5 she took her oath and simultaneously sued the SC; June 25 the SC (J. Jose Portugal Perez, 7-4) affirmed the Comelec, citing the HRET-three-element Member threshold (proclamation + oath + June 30 assumption-of-office), and held that the Comelec still had jurisdiction when it decided on May 14 (which became final on May 19, before Reyes met all three HRET-Member elements). The resolution had legal and logical moorings and cited many precedents ├óŌé¼ŌĆØ with due respect, he cannot understand why it ignited a congressional conflagration. He delivers the rule-of-law maxim: legislators have the right to disagree but not to disobey. He discloses his own three-stage practice: he disagreed many times as AJ, persuaded colleagues internally as CJ but did not begrudge those who differed, and as retired jurist has critiqued flip-flops but always honored and followed decisions. Congress is not helpless ├óŌé¼ŌĆØ it holds the purse, legislates laws, has oversight powers, determines court jurisdictions, can change jurisprudence, and most importantly can impeach justices ├óŌé¼ŌĆØ but only for culpable violation of the Constitution, treason, bribery, graft and corruption, other high crimes, or betrayal of public trust, NOT for their decisions, opinions, or votes, even if these may later turn out to be erroneous. By the same token, lawmakers can be disciplined only for disorderly behavior, not for bills and votes, even if these may later prove unwise.

one_paragraph_summary: Panganiban writes one of his rare biographical-disclosure columns, summarizing his Mapa High School Alumni Association speech delivered the previous Sunday ŌĆö Feb 23, 2014, at Metro Tent Pasig before 1,000 alumni. 'Please talk of how you became a sterling example of what a true-blue Mapan should be and how we can give back to society,' was the request of former Cong. Benny Abante Jr., president of the Mapa High School Alumni Association ŌĆö CJ's full speech may be accessed at cjpanganiban.com; here's a short version to fit in this space. Mostly poor were the Mapa students of CJ's time; we looked at education and hard work as our keys to success ŌĆö CJ had to hawk newspapers, peddle cigarettes and shine shoes to support his study; tired and weary, CJ promised himself he would one day be president of the Manila Times, the largest-circulated daily then. After high school the UP granted CJ a scholarship, but his impoverished parents could not afford the then 15-centavo bus fare to the UP campus in Quezon City. The nearby UST refused CJ a scholarship because he failed to answer the last of three questions of an old Dominican priest: first, 'How many Gods are there?'; second, 'How many persons are there in one God?'; and third, the tough question, 'Name them,' to which CJ sheepishly answered, 'Susmaryosep (Jesus, Mary and Joseph)!' ŌĆö yes, CJ was a religious ignoramus. So CJ enrolled at FEU, which gave him a scholarship without any question; CJ wanted to be a chemical engineer but his strict father ordered him to take up law. Inconsolable was CJ's grief at his inability to enroll at UP and to study chemical engineering, but his life turned at FEU ŌĆö there CJ met the brilliant law dean Jovito R. Salonga, who taught CJ his insights from the three schools he studied in: UP, Harvard and Yale. After graduating cum laude and placing sixth in the bar exam, CJ was granted a scholarship at Yale; but, like his UP problem, he did not have transport money ŌĆö so, he forthwith practiced and taught law, and dabbled in business; CJ organized Baron Travel Corp., which enabled his five children to travel, and finish their graduate degrees at Harvard, Stanford, U. of California, U. of Chicago, and U. of Michigan. CJ also intensified his study of his Catholic faith ŌĆö as a result, he was invited to the Second Plenary Council of the Philippines; later, he was named by Pope John Paul II as the only Filipino member for the 1996-2001 term of the Pontifical Council for the Laity in the Vatican; in 1991, he became part-time president of the Inquirer. Supreme Court journey: like his entry into the law profession, CJ did not plan his journey to the SC. On June 17, 1992, then President-elect Fidel V. Ramos offered CJ to be his Secretary of Justice; CJ respectfully declined, saying, 'Thank you, Mr. President, but I am not worthy to join your Cabinet; I did not campaign for you during the last elections.' However, FVR was insistent: 'I know you did not campaign for me; in fact, you did not even vote for me; you voted for Jovy Salonga; but that is all right; I want a nonpartisan Cabinet.' After two weeks, FVR finally relented: 'Ah, sa Supreme Court ka na lang.' A decade later, President GMA named CJ the 21st Chief Justice of the Philippines. As a jurist, CJ espoused his legal philosophy of 'Liberty and Prosperity Under the Rule of Law' ŌĆö CJ believes justice and jobs, freedom and food, ethics and economics, democracy and development, and liberty and prosperity must always go together; one is useless without the other. After his retirement in December 2006, he embarked on a new journey as an independent director or adviser of some of the largest firms listed in the PSE, and also writes a column in the Inquirer. Giving back: CJ asks his successful co-alumni to give back to society by sharing their time, talent and treasure ŌĆö the sharing of time and talent is just as important as the sharing of treasure; no one is so poor he cannot share something, and no one is so rich he does not need anything. CJ gives back his time and talent by being chair or president of several foundations, like the Manila Cathedral Foundation, Metrobank Foundation, and FLP. The sharing of treasure should really remain anonymous, so CJ will just mention two items already of public knowledge: to celebrate his 75th birthday two years ago, CJ gave half of his retirement pay to FLP and his one-year income as a columnist to the Inquirer Foundation for Newsboys; and today, may CJ contribute eight subsistence scholarships for poor Mapans. To conclude, education, hard work and God transformed CJ from a poor newsboy to being president of the most widely-read newspaper in our country; from an ignorant Catholic to being a member of the highest lay council of the Church in the Vatican; from a frustrated applicant for graduate studies to being father of five wonderful children who each achieved CJ's impossible dream of graduating from a pedigreed US university; from a fumbling shoeshine boy to the boardrooms of the best corporate giants; from an aspiring chemical engineer to a reluctant lawyer; and, finally, to the highest judicial position in our country. As CJ contemplates his life and moves toward its sunset, he knows that God has woven his many pains and gains into a magnificent tapestry showing His mystical presence ŌĆö truly, there is one constancy in his life: the presence, care and providence of our Lord Jesus Christ; to Him CJ dedicates all he was, all he is, and all he will ever be.

one_paragraph_summary: Panganiban opens the three-part bar-exam-and-legal-education trilogy with this first installment. Conducted annually on the four Sundays of October, the bar examinations determine who will be blessed to carry the exalted title of 'attorney-at-law' and be allowed to practice law in all courts in our country. Bar mystique: the Professional Regulation Commission (PRC) supervises the entry to all professions in the Philippines, except the legal profession which is reserved by the Constitution for the Supreme Court. The PRC-administered tests are called 'licensure examinations.' However, the crucible of lawyers is referred to as the 'bar' exam, not because they are more addicted to alcoholic drinks, but because only those who pass it may cross the courtroom railing or bar that separates the lawyers' table and the judges' bench, on one hand, from the seats used by the general public, on the other. Hence, lawyers are collectively referred to as the 'bar' and judges, the 'bench.' The bar exam used to be attended by fanfare galore. Undergraduates held parades and motorcades complete with marching bands and cheering squads that accompanied their school candidates' rides to the exam venue. This gaudy practice had been banned by the Supreme Court during the last several years. But the mystique explodes when the results are released. Traditional and social media herald the passers, complete with the pictures of the topnotchers plastered on the front pages of newspapers and interviewed over radio and television. Law schools take enormous pride in their bar placers and average passing rates. One institution, the University of Cebu, gifts its top 10 with brand-new cars. The most prestigious law firms and best-managed companies offer fantastic opportunities and compensation to the top placers. My personal experience: CJ took the bar exam in 1960 without this opening-day fanfare. In fact, on the first day of the exam, he had to wade alone in rain-induced, knee-deep flood on R. Papa Street, Sampaloc, Manila, which connected the then Far Eastern University Hospital (where he was confined for intestinal flu) and the University of the East, the situs of the exam. Because of his illness, he felt he may have failed the first-day subjects and seriously thought of quitting. But his dean, Dr. Jovito R. Salonga, pepped him up and urged him to go on. Recalling this incident in a speech on Jan 22, 2008, at FEU, Dr. Salonga opined that 'CJ Panganiban could have landed first place (instead of sixth) had he not been sick and hospitalized during the first week of the 1960 bar exam.' CJ mentions this because he believes that placing in the top 10 is not always a reliable measure of achievement. In fact, a classmate and CJ compared their answers after each exam day. CJ was so impressed with his friend's answers that he expected him to fare better than CJ. The point is that the questions during CJ's time called mostly for essay answers and memory work. CJ thought his classmate's citation of authorities was more impressive than his own. But apparently, the examiner's evaluation of their answers was quite subjective and probably veered toward CJ's essayed opinions. Hence, his grades were higher. Exam scope and methodology: the subjective methodology and scope of the bar exam have not improved much since CJ took it. The basic subjects are the same, except that for this year, two subjects ŌĆö taxation and labor law ŌĆö were added, and the methodology changed to 20 percent multiple choice questions (MCQ) and 80 percent essay. Inquirer publisher Raul C. Pangalangan lamented in his column on Nov 25, 2011, that 'the bar exams have been administered on basically the same template... The model remained unchanged at least for the last 50 years...' To be fair, however, let it be said that during the last decade, the Supreme Court tried to improve the bar exam. Thus, in a resolution issued on June 8, 2004 (Bar Matter 1161), the Court favorably acted on the reforms proposed by retired Justices Vicente V. Mendoza, Ameurfina M. Herrera and Jose C. Vitug (see CJ's April 22, 2007 column for details). Moreover, the Court instituted more reforms in another resolution on Jan 18, 2011, with MCQ at 60 percent of the total exams to measure the 'knowledge and recall of laws, doctrines and jurisprudence' of the candidates plus 40 percent essay-type questions to test their 'skill in writing in English, sorting out the relevant facts in a legal dispute... organizing [their] thoughts, constructing [their] arguments, and persuading readers to [their] point of view.' However, the essays were not 'bar subject specific,' igniting fiery howling from the candidates. In 2012, the Supreme Court reverted to the 'bar subject specific' essays at 40 percent and MCQs at 60 percent. In the 2013 exam, this was changed to 20 percent MCQ and 80 percent essay ŌĆö the same format for next month's exam, chaired by Justice Diosdado M. Peralta. Postscript: the Supreme Court unanimously voided the Commission on Elections' edict reducing TV-radio ads during elections to an 'aggregate total' of only 120 minutes for TV and 180 minutes for radio per national candidate. As CJ argued in his Feb 24, 2013 column, this 'draconian reduction suppressed the people's right to know their candidates, and the aspirants' rights to inform the electorate of their qualifications and programs of government.' The good news, however, is that the Comelec accepted its defeat graciously and will not ask for reconsideration. And the better news is that it will, as it should, enforce strictly the legal limits on the candidates' campaign.
