one_paragraph_summary: Panganiban opens by noting that Christmas and the New Year are grand occasions for family bonding and rejoicing ŌĆö last Sunday he featured a sharing by grandson Miguel P. Sandejas (C99); today he presents an essay by another grandson, Rafa P. Yaptangco, a fourth-year student at Southridge School in Alabang submitted to secure college admission at Ateneo de Manila University. Rafa's essay begins: 'Ageless passion is what propelled my grandfather, retired Chief Justice Artemio V. Panganiban, in his rise from rags to riches, not in worldly wealth but in his multi-career fulfillment. His life story is so compelling and so moving that it, deservedly so, was brought to glory in a musicale for his 75th birthday celebration.' The opening message of the musicale comes from one of Lolo's favorite stories ŌĆö about a farmer and his 20-year-old son experiencing good fortune, then bad fortune, then good fortune again, back and forth. The whole village would go to the farmer and marvel at his good fortune or show empathy at his bad fortune; and to everything, the farmer simply answered, 'Good news, bad news, who knows? I will just keep on doing my best and let God do the rest.' This has now become Rafa's mantra in life. Thinking back, Rafa never really appreciated who Lolo was until asked to represent the younger version of him in that musicale, titled 'Ageless Passion.' Cringing at fear of singing to a large audience, he politely turned down that opportunity and settled for a minor role; in hindsight he should have taken the challenge. During the experience he saw a different perspective he would not have been able to experience in his immediate society: poverty, both in the storyline and amongst the performers ŌĆö most notably the choir, composed of 20 or so boys performing to raise funds for their school. The musicale tells of CJP's transformations: from newsboy from the backstreets of Sampaloc, Manila to the presidency of the Philippine Daily Inquirer; from an ignorant Catholic to an advisor to the Holy Father in the Vatican; from a frustrated applicant for graduate studies to father of five wonderful children who each achieved his impossible dream of graduating from a pedigreed US university; from a shoeshine boy to the boardrooms of the largest blue chips in our part of Planet Earth; and from a frustrated chemical engineer to a reluctant lawyer to the highest magistrate of the land. Rafa was taken aback at the story of Lolo's life ŌĆö back then all he really thought of him was a caring father to his Mom who loved (and still does) to take the family out to Sunday dinners. Upon really understanding what Lolo has gone through, it absolutely amazed Rafa. It is incredibly hard to believe that all this was achieved by a single person ŌĆö and it was done by Lolo. It was then Rafa realized what character he had and the ageless passion he has radiated; it was then Lolo became his idol, the person he hopes to eventually be like, the person for whom he has infinite respect. He shows that anyone willing to contribute a great deal of hard work for a better future can, indeed, have a better future. Rafa is extremely lucky to have been born into circumstances where finance is not a problem and infinitely grateful to be the grandson of a man so industrious. Following Lolo's life story, Rafa has set out with the general goal: to make Lolo proud. This threw life around, as he did not really have a life goal in mind at that time; to just be successful is not enough. Rafa now sees a light at the end of the tunnel: to employ the ageless passion Lolo has taught him through his life story. Even now, Lolo continues to work hard as he is in the board of numerous big-name companies; most of the things he talks about at the dinner table are about law and business ŌĆö this just goes to show how dedicated to his work he is, and Rafa strives to someday achieve that. Rafa has gone on to cofound the Admirals Business Club of Southridge, managing numerous concerts including one to raise awareness for cancer patients in partnership with Moving as One (MAO), money earned given to charity. He has also cofounded L├®ger de Main, a cardistry, illusions, and deceptions club, teaching people card tricks and other forms of magic ŌĆö hoping to instill values of confidence and composure in public performances. As leader he has driven both groups to be more unique organizations in Southridge, setting up a leadership-handoff system to ensure continuity post-graduation. In all this Rafa is thankful for Southridge's philosophy of forming men of integrity, grateful for his parents, older brother, relatives, and good friends ŌĆö but most of all knows he must not stray from the valuable lesson of the farmer in Lolo's story. Already he can see himself on the right track to making Lolo proud. Obviously, it will not be a straight path to the goal; there will be highs and lows. 'Good news, bad news, who knows? I will just keep doing my best and let God do the rest' ŌĆö circular bookend to the farmer-parable opening.

one_paragraph_summary: Panganiban reproduces his response speech at the December 2021 launch of 'With Due Respect 3,' the third five-year compilation of his Inquirer columns published by Marixi R. Prieto and her daughter Sandy Prieto-Romualdez (Inquirer Group President and CEO). He recounts how, despite a youthful 'historical romance with newspapers' as a Sampaloc paper-peddler, he had never written a column until after retiring from the judiciary and was very hesitant when Marixi invited him ŌĆö especially after Dr. Jovito R. Salonga told him 'Chief Justices make history; they do not write it,' to which Marixi answered, 'Dr. Salonga may be correct, but I do not know of any retired chief justice who had been offered to write a column.' He has written every Sunday for 15 years since February 2007 ŌĆö longer than his 11+ years on the Court ŌĆö and credits Marixi for whatever accolades have followed, including WDR1's third place on Amazon's bestseller list (topped only by Jeffrey Toobin at #1 and Antonin Scalia at #2). He thanks incumbent CJ Alexander G. Gesmundo for launching WDR3 ŌĆö Gesmundo at 65 could be CJ Panganiban's son ŌĆö and endorses him as embodying the four constitutional attributes (competence, integrity, probity, independence), 'a glutton for work' determined to solve the perennial SC backlog and automate the judiciary. He closes by thanking JV Rufino of the Inquirer Book Company; 'Compa├▒ero' Sean James Borja (Ateneo 2018 valedictorian and 2018 bar first placer) who arranged the columns topically rather than chronologically; and the editors Jorge Aruta, Rosario Garcellano (retired), and Gilbert Cadiz (incumbent opinion editor) for patience with his grammatical lapses.

one_paragraph_summary: Panganiban reproduces his graduation speech at the Far Eastern University-Nicanor Reyes Medical Foundation (FEU-NRMF), framed around miraculous escapes from death and two pieces of advice. He recounts being rushed to FEU Hospital in 1957 ŌĆö sophomore law-student president of the FEU Central Student Organization opposing a P6 hospitalization fee ŌĆö with a white-blood-cell count over 36,000; Dr. Ricardo Alfonso asked whether he wanted to pay the full bill or the six-peso fee, and CJ withdrew his opposition mid-pain; the rupture was at the rear-left (unusual) and had been contained for two weeks by enveloping fat. He recalls 1942 dysentery at age six during WWII with no hospital, medicine, doctor, or nurse ŌĆö his mother refused to surrender and he 'miraculously recovered in her arms.' In 1949 he jumped from a speeding jeepney while peddling newspapers and a 6├Ś6 army truck rolled over him; he survived because his lean body slid under the high road clearance. In 1983 he was diagnosed with diabetes (still seen every three months by Dr. Augusto Litonjua); he had three hemorrhoid operations and heart-rhythm skips (Dr. Dante Morales confirmed a rare natural skip). He attributes all to Jesus and Mary and to FEU chaplain Fr. Michael Nolan who first taught him the Our Father, Hail Mary, Glory Be, and FEU Prayer (the last a source of the 'Centennial Prayer of the Courts' composed in 2001). His first advice: 'Always pray and trust God' ŌĆö but pray not to banish evils, only for strength to overcome them, since 'God will always help us ŌĆö in His own way, in His own time.' His second advice: 'Work with two Cs ŌĆö competence and care' ŌĆö illustrated by an unnamed Filipino multibillionaire confined in a top US hospital and treated 'merely as an object to be studied, operated on, and monitored,' longing for the companionship of Filipino doctors and nurses. He closes with the synthesis: 'Pray as though everything depended on God; work as though everything depended on you.'

one_paragraph_summary: Panganiban accepts the Manila Overseas Press Club's 'Journalist of the Year ŌĆö Law' award at the Oct 25, 2023 Fairmont Hotel Makati ceremony ŌĆö the first such MOPC awards in 78 years ŌĆö with the citation honoring his 'prolific work and prodigious excellence in writing about law and justice with depth, clarity, and command of language, thereby strengthening the people's faith in the third and often misunderstood branch of government, the judiciary' (and noting in passing that the cited '12 books' should be 14). He attributes the recognition to three 'Mega Media Movers': MOPC chair Antonio 'Tony' Lopez, retired Inquirer chair Marixi R. Prieto, and GMA Network chair/CEO Felipe 'Henry' L. Gozon. He declines the title 'Journalist' (reserved for 'battle-tested stars of the journalism profession') and self-describes as 'ACCIDENTAL WRITER ŌĆö tsamba lang 'yon'; when Tony asked what he thought of the citation, CJ replied 'Supercalifragilisticexpialidocious!' ŌĆö the column's title and a self-aware critique that lawyers 'tend to be highfalutin, muddled, and long-worded' while journalists write clearly and simply, which he tries to imitate. He admires the Italian bell trophies (15 inches, three kilos, from a thousand-year-old bell-making tradition) and the co-honor of Inquirer's Dax Lucas as Business Writer of the Year. He recounts Marixi's late-1980s entry into the Inquirer as newsprint seller to 'a giant in circulation but a dwarf in money,' her P10 million convertible loan, and the two-bloc deadlock compromise that made her the 'ACCIDENTAL OWNER' ŌĆö paralleling his own 'accidental writer' description. After his 2006 retirement Marixi invited him to write thrice weekly; Dr. Jovito R. Salonga objected with 'I do not know of any CJ who became a columnist. Chief justices do not write history; they make it'; Marixi parried 'I do not know of any chief justice who had been offered to write a regular column' and 'won the battle of wits,' leading to CJ's 17-year weekly run (longer than his 11 years on the SC) ŌĆö without fail, without vacation, despite COVID in December 2022, despite Leni's unexpected death on April 9, 2023 (whose eulogy column became Inquirer.net's most-visited as of August 2023), and despite international travel. The first WDR volume in 2011 placed third in Amazon's courts-category bestseller list (bested only by Jeffrey Toobin of CNN and Justice Antonin Scalia of the US Supreme Court); CJ donated royalties to the Inquirer Newsboy Foundation. He then turns to Henry Gozon as 'our foremost expert on aviation law,' recalling their 50-year-ago clashes between Gozon as Philippine Airlines counsel and CJ as KLM and Continental counsel ŌĆö 'clashed passionately but socialized privately.' CJ repeatedly invited Gozon to the SC during his incumbency; Gozon declined. On Dec 6, 2006 ŌĆö CJ's day of SC 'graduation' ŌĆö Gozon and GMA president Jimmy Duavit visited him at home to invite him to the GMA board, and CJ 'became a witness to why he declined' as Gozon built GMA into 'the largest, most trusted, and best TV-radio network long before ABS-CBN was closed' ŌĆö today unrivaled per Nielsen with ratings higher than the 10 other major networks combined and a record P7.5B+ 2021 net profit, under the motto 'Sa totoo lang tayo.' He closes: 'I feel like receiving an honorary Ph.D. from the MOPC University of Journalism.'

one_paragraph_summary: On the closure of the three-day 2024 Bar exam (Sept 8, 11, 15), Panganiban reflects on a profession that must 'swing on reluctant hinges.' His own 1960 batch was 4,178 with 1,667 passers, held over four consecutive Sundays at the University of the East; yearly 1965-1981 cohorts numbered under 2,000; the lowest pass rate was 16.59% in 1999, when an attempt to lower the passing grade from 75 to 74 percent was scuttled by CJ's strong objections ŌĆö and the bar-exam chair was reprimanded and his honorarium halved for failing to disclose that a nephew of his was a taker. The most numerous batch was 2021's 11,402 in the first digitized regionalized exam, with a record 8,241 passers at 72.28% and (for the first time) the top 10 unnamed. With due respect, CJ disagrees with this wholesale entry ŌĆö the legal profession is the first key to the justice system and lawyers are public officers ŌĆö and with the nondisclosure of top 10 placers (since abandoned), because while bar topping is no guarantee of excellence, top 10 placers nonetheless become leading practitioners, professors, corporate counsels, and government officials. Of the 27 Philippine CJs (excluding ousted-via-quo-warranto Sereno, deemed never a de jure CJ), at least 11 are bar placers: Yulo (3rd), Paras (2nd), Bengzon (2nd), Concepcion (1st), Makalintal (7th), R. Aquino (9th), Teehankee (1st), Yap (1st), Narvasa (2nd), Panganiban (6th), and Bersamin (9th). Since student days, CJ has idealized three bar legends: Jose W. Diokno and Jovito R. Salonga, tied at 95.3% in 1944 ŌĆö and amazingly Diokno did not finish law school (and also topped the CPA boards); Salonga was CJ's FEU mentor, law-office boss, and lifetime guru, in awe of only Diokno and Teehankee ('Please prepare our trial brief extra well because Pepe Diokno or Dingdong Teehankee is our opponent'); and Claudio Teehankee, Ateneo summa BA and LLB and 1940 bar #1, who remained independent despite his Marcos appointment ŌĆö 'in my humble view, the greatest Ateneo law alumnus of all time.' In emulation of Salonga's 1986 PCGG-appointment law-firm dissolution, CJ dissolved his own firm Panganiban, Parlade, Benitez, and Africa on his 1995 SC appointment, orphaning Dean Agustin Benitez (1959 #1), Dean Custodio Parlade (1959 #4), and classmate Leopoldo Africa (1960 #11). 'Please excuse my feeble attempts to imitate these legends of the bar as I humbly try, though undeserving, to live in the penumbra of their great shadows.'

one_paragraph_summary: Responding to reader comments on his Oct 27 column 'Agreeing to disagree with Justice Carpio' ŌĆö where he described enjoying '95-percent harmony and overlooking our 5-percent cacophony' with Carpio ŌĆö Panganiban explains why his best friends are his best critics. He has 'differed without being difficult with all, repeat all, of my best friends,' and is grateful for their good-faith divergences expressed in gentle though sometimes emphatic language. He distinguishes this sharply from offensive criticisms at imagined defects conceived in malice; those he ignores, refusing to let 'schemers and kibitzers' disrupt his goals, and instead trusting his track record and the judgment of those who truly know him. His scriptural refuge is Romans 12:17-19 ŌĆö do not repay evil for evil, live at peace with all, leave vengeance to the Lord. He then cites three close friends as exemplars. First, the late Jovito R. Salonga ŌĆö his 'guru, dean and surrogate father,' bar topper, UP LLB plus Harvard LLM plus Yale JSD, three-time senatorial topper, 'the best president our country never had' ŌĆö set against CJ's self-deprecation as 'poor newsboy born in squalid Tondo, Manila' with an FEU LLB and bar 6th place. Salonga's foremost teaching was about life, not law: the rare gems money CANNOT buy ŌĆö excellence, integrity, honor, dignity, reverential regard for God. At the Jan 22, 2008 launch of Salonga and Miranda-Feliciano's 'A Test of Courage' (with Carpio as guest speaker), Salonga publicly compared CJ to St. Paul on the road to Damascus ŌĆö inward journey, blinding light, transformation ŌĆö and confessed 'In a deeper sense, Art is my mentor.' Yet they 'agreed to disagree on something very fundamental': Salonga was a revered Cosmopolitan Church pastor, CJ a fledgling Catholic. Second, Archbishop Leonardo Z. Legaspi ŌĆö as CBCP president, the presiding officer at PCP II in mid-Feb 1991 ŌĆö repeatedly ruled CJ out of order for proposing that priests and nuns be allowed to marry; yet despite CJ's stubbornness, Legaspi (with Ambassador Tita de Villa) recommended him to Pope John Paul II, who named CJ 'though unworthy' as the only Filipino member of the Pontifical Council of the Laity (1996-2001). Third, his wife Leni Panganiban critiques his writing, speech, looks and even dress ŌĆö yet remains his closest friend and beloved, because every critical word is uttered with unconditional love. The closing universal principle: 'Friend first before critic, and love first before criticism.'

one_paragraph_summary: Panganiban opens with the 2024 pro-Palestinian campus rallies ignited at Columbia University, spreading within days to 30+ other US universities and then to France, the UK, Norway, and Australia ŌĆö influencing US insistence that Israel temper its Gaza attacks on civilian enclaves and back a ceasefire with hostage release. He laments that the students' ranks were 'infiltrated by outside actors' that converted free speech into vandalism and violence, drawing NYC police in full force; he pities 'the poor, victimized students of Columbia and other universities' who sacrificed time, meager resources, possible suspension or expulsion. They remind him of his own late-1950s activism as president of the FEU Central Student Organization (FEUCSO) and the National Union of Students of the Philippines (NUSP) ŌĆö rallying private nonsectarian schools and battling to rouse the Catholic and 'cloistered' women's colleges (Ateneo, La Salle, St. Theresa, St. Scholastica, Maryknoll/Miriam) in parallel with UP's Fernando Lagua, Homobono Adaza, and Emmanuel Soriano. CJ was then infatuated with Karl Marx's 'From each according to his ability, and to each according to his needs' as the solution to poverty, but two people dissuaded him: FEU chaplain Fr. Michael Nolan, who taught him catechesis and challenged him to confront the economic realities of Red China and Soviet Russia ('while communism may be theoretically attractive, it was not implementable'), and Elenita A. Carpio, editor of the 'Scholastican' and aspiring Benedictine nun, his 'accidental' seatmate on a 10-hour Manila-Naga train ride to a NUSP seminar. Over three years of visits to her family home, she converted him to Christ and eventually married him 'to the chagrin of the St. Scho nuns who expected her to join them in blessed singlehood.' 'My Leni tenderly opened the clenched fist of this activist,' redirecting him to the judiciary, the private sector, family, and the Lord ŌĆö later leading to his appointment by Pope John Paul II to the Pontifical Council for the Laity (1996-2002). He traces PH student activism's origin to Wenceslao Q. Vinzons (1941 resistance to the Japanese invasion), through the late-1950s peacetime organizing he led, to the 1970 First Quarter Storm (Jan 26-March 17) led by NUSP's Edgar Jopson, UPSC's Jerry Barican, and Kabataang Makabayan's Sison and Ocampo against Marcos Sr. He predicts that current student leaders are 'not yet' engaged with Israel-Hamas, Russia-Ukraine, or West Philippine Sea ŌĆö but will eventually take a stand and resume student activism in the Philippines.

one_paragraph_summary: Panganiban delivers an Easter Sunday column structured around sad news and good news about the Manila Cathedral. The sad news: Archbishop Luis Antonio G. Tagle ordered the cathedral closed a month earlier on the recommendation of the Manila Cathedral-Basilica Foundation (MCBF) after Dr. Angel Lazaro III's engineering team ŌĆö testing slabs, beams, columns, walls, footings, piles, trusses, domes, parapets, and cables with 30-meter bore holes ŌĆö found that soil liquefaction could occur in a major earthquake, risking the well-being of the faithful, the 200 couples scheduled to be married there in 2012, and the annual December pipe-organ concerts featuring the giant organ that George Miller's 1912 'Interesting Manila' once called 'the most modern in the Philippines.' Cardinal Rosales had already suspected deterioration, taking down the seven 17-ton belfry bells with New Golden City Builders and architect William Coscolluela, replacing them with Holland-made computerized carillon bells donated by Alberto and Sylvia Lina ŌĆö but even these lighter bells must now also come down. The good news: at a private dinner with Archbishop Tagle, former Ambassador to the Vatican Henrietta T. de Villa, and CJ, San Miguel Corp. president Ramon S. Ang 'graciously agreed to contribute P50 million' to lead the rehabilitation. CJ celebrates the cathedral as 'more than mortar and brick' ŌĆö a symbol of Filipino Catholicism standing since 1581 through typhoons, earthquakes, fire, and WWII; reconstructed in neo-Romanesque Byzantine form by Fernando Ocampo under Archbishop Rufino J. Santos (first Filipino cardinal), consecrated Dec 7, 1958, and elevated to basilica by Pope John Paul II in 1982. He lists the foundation officers (himself as president, Marixi R. Prieto VP, Msgr. Nestor C. Cerbo treasurer/executive director, Alex Erlito S. Fider corporate secretary, board members Cortez, Asuncion, Sescon, Guidote-Alvarez, Dolor, Oppen, Syquia) and expects 'more of our countrymen would come forward with contributions, big and small' to rebuild and beautify the center of worship in 'the only Catholic country in Asia.'

one_paragraph_summary: Panganiban recounts his speaking engagement at the Third Philippine Conference on New Evangelization (PCNE III) at the UST Quadricentennial Pavilion (July 15-17, 2016), attended by over 5,000 faithful, where Cardinal Tagle invited him to speak on how Catholics can evangelize in governance and public service. He clarifies that the doctrine of separation of church and state does NOT prohibit clergy and laity from advocating pro-Christian tenets ŌĆö it merely requires government neutrality in religious 'competition' (no public funds for one group's basilica, no promotion of one's dogma, no salaries for strictly religious duties, no religion as condition for civil/political right). While there is separation of church FROM state, there is no separation of the STATE FROM GOD: the Philippines is theist, monotheist ŌĆö not atheist, not even agnostic ŌĆö as evidenced by the constitutional preamble 'We, the sovereign Filipino people, imploring the aid of Almighty God,' the prayer-prefacing practices of both Congress chambers and the Cabinet, and the 'Centennial Prayer for the Courts' used by the Supreme Court. Constitution and laws do not bar priests and nuns from public office; only Canon Law sometimes limits clerical political participation. As Catholics, the bearings come from Pope Francis's Evangelii Gaudium No. 182: 'The Church's pastors, taking into account the contributions of the different sciences, have the right to offer opinions on all that affects people's lives, since the task of evangelization implies and demands the integral promotion of each human being. It is no longer possible to claim that religion should be restricted to the private sphere and that it exists only to prepare souls for heaven.' The Catechism and Vatican II urge bringing Gospel values into public and private life. The most crucial temporal matter is federalism, which requires constitutional overhaul (not amendment) ŌĆö first challenge is method (Con-ass vs Con-con) and second is making the new Constitution 'as pro-God, pro-faith, pro-life, pro-prayer, pro-family and pro-human rights as possible,' with vigilance from method-decision through delegate-election, deliberations, and plebiscite. The proposed revival of death penalty (by hanging or musketry) and summary execution of alleged criminals without due process must be resisted ŌĆö 'we know that the end never justifies the means.' Catholics should support (without violating fundamental rights) the drive against criminality, drugs, and corruption; calamity relief; free education for the poor; job creation with just wages; the peace process; and credible elections. Civil servants may need to use 'freedom of speech to assert, to argue and to denounce' and uphold Christian values 'at the risk of ridicule, censure, or even of losing our jobs.' He closes: 'in all our trials and triumphs, defeats and victories, frustrations and exaltations, pains and gains, crucifixions and resurrections, God will never abandon us. He will always be with us: above us to inspire us, behind us to strengthen us, before us to lead us, and within us to transform us to be another Christ ŌĆö humble, loving, and sacrificing.'

one_paragraph_summary: Panganiban gives a fair-minded close reading of Judge Rainelda H. Estacio-Montesa's 37-page decision (fortified by 170 footnotes) convicting Maria Ressa and Reynaldo Santos Jr. of cyberlibel ├óŌé¼ŌĆØ finding it 'not baseless or capricious' but appealable to the Supreme Court. He walks through the trial sequence (arraignment, mediation, pretrial, six prosecution witnesses including Wilfredo Keng, two defense witnesses) and identifies the trial court's analytical-school architecture: 'law times facts equals decision,' Austin-style. He explains three crucial doctrinal items. First, the article publicly imputed despicable crimes (human trafficking, drug/contraband smuggling, murder) tending to dishonor Keng, a private citizen; under Article 353/355 and the Cybercrime Law (which extends libel to 'computer system or similar means' with heavier penalty), MALICE IS PRESUMED BY LAW, shifting the burden onto the accused ├óŌé¼ŌĆØ and 'lamentably, the defense miserably failed in this regard,' with both accused 'oddly also' opting not to testify on fair-comment-on-public-interest. Second, the retroactivity defense fails: the Information charged the February 19, 2014 republication (post-Cybercrime Law), not the May 2012 original, and every republication is a separate crime. Third, on prescription, Montesa used John Austin's robotic syllogism ├óŌé¼ŌĆØ because the Cybercrime Law prescribes 6+ years' imprisonment without setting a new prescriptive period, Act No. 3326's twelve-year rule applies. CJ concludes that the article IS libelous, but the accused can still be acquitted on appeal IF the Supreme Court sidesteps Austin's strict syllogism and embraces Roscoe Pound's more liberal sociological school, which 'regards law merely as a brick ├óŌé¼ŌĆØ an important one ├óŌé¼ŌĆØ in the building of a just and humane society,' reading the one-year libel prescription back into cyberlibel under the rule that penal laws should favor the accused. He closes with a tribute: 'Tomorrow, June 22, is the 100th birthday of the late Dr. Jovito R. Salonga, my esteemed guru, a respected scholar, low-profile educator, brilliant lawyer, outstanding lawmaker, acclaimed patriot, and statesman par excellence.'

one_paragraph_summary: Panganiban announces the first batch of LibPros law scholars ŌĆö originally planned at 10 but expanded to 11 after the board of judges, led by CJ Maria Lourdes P. A. Sereno, was so impressed it recommended an extra scholar; the FLP happily agreed. The Dec 1, 2016 awarding ceremony at the new UP campus in Fort Bonifacio, Taguig will feature CJ Sereno as guest speaker; each scholar receives full scholarship (tuition + books + monthly stipend up to P200,000), and 10 new scholars will be selected every school year. The distribution across schools: Ateneo, FEU-Makati, San Beda, and UST 2 each; PUP, USC, UP 1 each ŌĆö named: Sean James Borja, Kaycelle Anne Castillo, Ervin Frederick Dy, Rexlyn Ann Evora, Ken Kevin Ganchero, Jose Noel Hilario, Violeta Najarro Jr., Dion Ceasar Pascua, Ma Janine Pedernal, Tess Marie Tan, and Vanessa Gloria Vergara. The program is cosponsored by the Tan Yan Kee Foundation (TYKF) in cooperation with the Philippine Association of Law Schools (PALS); the board of judges includes CJ Sereno, FLP president Evelyn T. Dumdum, former education secretary Edilberto C. de Jesus, TYKF legal counsel Ma Cecilia L. Pesayco, and PALS president Ma Soledad D. Mawis, with FLP executive director Martin Angelo L. Esguerra assisting. Qualification requires being a third-year law student in an eligible school, top 10% of class, Ōēź83% (2.25) average across four prior semesters, full loads with no drops, dean's commendation, an essay on liberty and prosperity, and board screening. The FLP was organized in December 2011 during CJ's 75th birthday to perpetuate the legal philosophy that 'justice and jobs, freedom and food, ethics and economics, democracy and development, nay, liberty and prosperity must always go together; one is useless without the other.' Beyond the scholarships, FLP and the Ayala Group cosponsor a dissertation contest open to all law students nationwide (P300K champion, P200K runner-up, P100K each for 3 honorable mentions, P20K each for 20 qualifying entries); with Metrobank Foundation and MPIC, FLP has created 10 professorial chairs ŌĆö 9 already filled by the law deans of Ateneo, FEU, USC, UP, UST, DLSU, Silliman, U Cordilleras, plus the PHILJA chancellor; USC dean Joan Largo will deliver her fourth professorial lecture in Cebu City on Dec 9. FLP acquired its Makati head office with assistance from First Philippine Holdings, Lopez Holdings, Meralco, PLDT, MPIC, and San Miguel's Ramon S. Ang. A postscript clarifies last Sunday's column on US elector selection: each state determines elector election methods by local law ŌĆö in some, elector names are on the ballot; in others, presidential candidate names appear but votes still count for the electors, not the candidates directly.

one_paragraph_summary: Panganiban delivers a shortened version of his keynote speech at the FLP scholarship ceremony of late July 2023 ŌĆö where Foundation for Liberty and Prosperity awarded 21 legal scholarships (P200,000 each, Tan Yan Kee partnership), five Esmel fellowships (P450,000 each, Metro Pacific partnership), and ten dissertation winners (P1.325M total, Ayala Corp partnership), with CJ Alexander G. Gesmundo as keynote speaker. He frames life as 'a strange interplay of light and shadows, of victories and defeats' and offers his autobiography as evidence of the door-window principle: he won a UP scholarship but couldn't afford the 15-centavo Diliman ride; he therefore enrolled at nearby FEU where he was mentored by Jovito R. Salonga and Alejandro R. Roces, becoming the youngest FEUCSO president, founding NUSP, graduating cum laude, placing sixth in the bar ŌĆö only to be granted a Yale full scholarship that the US Embassy denied a visa for, due to his student activism. He buried himself as 'a virtual hermit' on the SC, producing 1,200+ fully signed decisions, one book per year, chairing most committees, and earning a unanimous Court resolution naming him 'The Renaissance Jurist of the 21st Century' and 'the Court's most prolific writer, bar none.' Five years after retirement, inspired by national artist Ryan Cayabyab's musical 'Ageless Passion,' he resolved to convert his philosophy from 'imbedded legal lore' into 'practical reality... outside the intellectual realm of lawyers into the world of hard knocks and grinding poverty.' He seeded FLP with P2M; companies and foundations he advised gave P50M for the Makati head office and another P50M+ for educational projects. He announces FLP's two 'ultimate projects': a Center for Liberty and Prosperity hosting an interactive, immersive, AI-powered Museum (visitors enter a virtual SC Session Hall and converse with AI robots mimicking justices) dedicated to the talented; and a multibillion-peso Prosperity Fund for MSMEs, nonprofit but financially viable, dedicated to the poor. He donated his family's P100M+ Paseo de Magallanes lot to seed the two projects, anchoring all this in Matthew 25:40: 'Whatever you do for the least of my brethren, you do unto me.'

one_paragraph_summary: Panganiban announces that the Foundation for Liberty and Prosperity (FLP), in partnership with the Metro Pacific Investments Foundation, is expanding from the liberty-only side of its philosophy to the prosperity side through the launch of 'Esmel Fellowships' ŌĆö Entrepreneurship, Sustainability, Management, Economics, business Law ŌĆö at P450,000 per grantee (P350,000 tuition + P100,000 books/stipend), strictly merit-based, awarded to five qualified applicants enrolled in MBA/MS-equivalent graduate programs at AIM, ADMU, DLSU, UP, or UST. The Legal Scholarship Program, now in its sixth year and partnered with the Tan Yan Kee Foundation, continues to award at least 20 of the best and brightest at P200,000 each (P100k tuition + P20k books + P80k stipend), open to third and fourth year students of law schools above the overall bar passing rate per the Office of the Bar Confidant. The Dissertation Writing Contest with Ayala Corporation augments the literature on liberty/prosperity under rule of law. He recites the rosters: 21 scholars named (10 third-year, 11 fourth-year) for the prior school year, with CJ Gesmundo having chaired the legal scholarship judges and Senior Justice Estela M. Perlas-Bernabe the dissertation judges. Winners of the dissertation contest were Cuntapay (ADMU, P320,000), Baliong (USC, P220,000), and three third-placers (De Vera DLSU, Dulanas USC, Lim ADMU) at P120,000 each. FLP ŌĆö founded 2011 with a board including De Jesus, Sandoval-Gutierrez, Tetangco, Gregorio, Prof. Elenita C. Panganiban (CJ's wife), Maria Elena P.S. Yaptangco, and Sean James Borja, with CJ as chair ŌĆö works toward two ultimate projects: an immersive interactive Museum for Liberty and Prosperity (liberty) and a multibillion-peso Entrepreneurship Fund to help the poor help themselves through private entrepreneurship (prosperity).

one_paragraph_summary: Panganiban announces the FLP scholarship awardees for school year 2021-2022 ŌĆö 21 law students at P200,000 each (P100k tuition + P80k stipend + P20k books) ŌĆö selected by a board chaired by CJ Alexander G. Gesmundo with Justice Secretary Menardo I. Guevarra, former education secretary Edilberto C. de Jesus, TYKF vice chairman Harry C. Tan, law dean Anna Maria D. Abad, and lawyer Joel Emerson J. Gregorio as members. He names all 10 juniors (Crisostomo FEU, Laza St. Louis U, Leron UP, Maurin U Cebu, Opina UST, Pacumio UST, Ramos U Cordilleras, Salazar FEU, Sibayan U Cordilleras, Taongan DLSU) and all 11 seniors (Dela Cruz Father Saturnino Urios U, Dumelod UST, Fomaneg UP, Macalino ADMU, Mappang U Cordilleras, Mata USC, Negado USC, Obaob USC, Omamalin USC, Soriano UP, Sy ADMU), plus the dissertation contest winners chaired by Senior Justice Estela M. Perlas-Bernabe (Cuntapay ADMU P320k first; Baliong USC P220k second; three third-placers De Vera DLSU, Dulanas USC, Lim ADMU at P120k each), seven additional finalists at P20k each, and the faculty advisers' cash prizes. He articulates the unique FLP selection criterion ŌĆö academic excellence PLUS commitment to the philosophy of liberty and prosperity under rule of law ŌĆö and his signature catechism: 'the intertwining relationship of justice and jobs; freedom and food; ethics and economics; liberty and prosperity. One is useless without the other.' Echoing FDR's four freedoms recast for the Philippines, he stresses 'the indispensability and inseparability of the freedom from tyranny and the freedom from poverty; and of the freedom from fear and the freedom from hunger ŌĆö to be attained and preserved under the rule of law in a democracy, not under the rule of force in a monocracy.' Awardees automatically join the FLP Scholars Society, from which 'will arise the new leaders of our country ŌĆö patriotic, talented, and trustworthy promoters of liberty and prosperity under the rule of law.'

one_paragraph_summary: Panganiban opens with a FVR (Fidel V. Ramos) anecdote: though CJ advised him he did not campaign or vote for FVR, the late president on June 17, 1992 ŌĆö before assuming office on June 30, 1992 ŌĆö offered him the DOJ secretaryship; CJ declined, so FVR changed the offer to a Supreme Court seat and 'never spoke or influenced me, in any manner, in the discharge of my duties,' fully respecting CJ's independence and probity 'even when I voted against his known political wishes' ŌĆö a 'gentleman-patriot' who deserves fuller treatment in another column. He then previews the August 11, 2022 FLP awards ceremony with CJ Gesmundo as guest of honor and speaker: 21 law scholars (TYKF partnership; 10 fourth-years and 11 third-years, all named with school affiliations) selected by Gesmundo's board (with De Jesus, TYKF VC Harry Tan, PALS president Dean Gemy Festin, and Gregorio); three 'Exemplary'-rated bar topnotchers (Domingo UCordilleras, Mata USC, Omamalin USC) at P100,000 each plus Latin honor graduates at P25k-P100k each; 10 dissertation winners (Ayala partnership) ŌĆö Patricia Anne Alarios AdMU (1st P320k), Breden Arcayos USC (2nd P220k), three third-placers at P120k each (Pineda AdMU, Mabutin UP, Carreon USC), with faculty adviser prizes (Habaradas P100k, Pandi P75k, Temprosa/Pangalangan/Felongco P50k each) and five finalists at P20k each ŌĆö selected by Justice Ramon Paul L. Hernando's board (with Solomon Hermosura of Ayala, PALS EVP Dean Maria Anna Abad, De Jesus, Gregorio); and the inaugural Esmel fellowships (MPIF partnership) at P450,000 each to John Paul Abrina MD (AIM), Jacob Barbosa (AIM), Aibar Rabi Rashad Bibi (DLSU), Jella Marice Salarda (UP), and George Martin Samaco III (AIM), selected by former BSP governor Amando M. Tetangco Jr.'s board (with MPIC CFO Cabal-Revilla, former BPI president Cezar Consing, Baron Travel's Marilen P.S. Yaptangco, and Gregorio). He notes that FLP's first decade (2011-2021) concentrated on liberty programs; its second decade (2021-2031) will include prosperity, starting with Esmel and culminating in (a) the Center for Liberty and Prosperity hosting the immersive Museum (SC partnership, liberty side) and (b) the at-least-P1-billion Entrepreneurship Fund investing in and helping manage MSMEs 'with the active assistance of its Esmel fellows' (prosperity side).

one_paragraph_summary: Panganiban prefaces today's FLP awards ceremony with a portrait of First Lady Louise Araneta-Marcos ŌĆö whose competence as practicing lawyer and law professor he had heard of before her husband's presidency, and whom he met formally on June 28, 2023 (Sistine Chapel reproductions exhibit) and Aug 22, 2023 (Toyota Philippines 35th anniversary), where she acted so spontaneously, occasionally holding his arm, as if they had known each other for years: 'INDEED, A REAL CHARMER.' Invited through compa├▒ero Mike Toledo (Chamber of Mines chair and president) for Aug 19, she sent a personalized video citing 'sacred' days and requested a reschedule to today, Sept 2. Three groups of awardees will receive prizes from her, guest speaker Manuel V. Pangilinan (MVP), and CJ as FLP chair: 20 law scholars at P200,000 each (Tan Yan Kee Foundation partnership represented by Lucio Tan III, 'the Stanford summa, president of PAL Holdings and LT Group'), with 10 third-year students (Alberto, Despi, Mirasol, Mutuc, Orajay, Paguia, Sy, Sobrevi├▒as, Te, Viernes) and 10 fourth-year students (Barraquias, Cerillo, David, Gruyal, Laza, Patricio, Aimiel and Allan Reyes, Salcedo, Sanders Jr.) named with their schools; five MBA Esmel fellows at P450,000 each (Metro Pacific Investments Foundation partnership; P350,000 tuition + P100,000 stipend) ŌĆö Ballestar, Lucas, Panlilio II, Tuquero, and Villanueva; and 20 dissertation contest winners (Ayala Corp partnership) ŌĆö Rachel Gella (USLS) first prize P320,000, Arvin Maceda (FEU) second P220,000, three third placers at P120,000 (De Guia, Loro, Quebrar), and 15 finalists at P20,000 each, with the five winning dissertations accessible at www.libpros.com. The three boards of judges were chaired by CJ Alexander G. Gesmundo (scholars), past BSP governor Amando M. Tetangco Jr. ŌĆö 'now chair of the largest conglomerate in our country, SM Investments Corp.' (Esmel fellows) ŌĆö and Justice Amy C. Lazaro-Javier (dissertations). UP Law Dean Darlene Marie Berberabe will be inducted into the FLP Professorial Chairs program by Metrobank chair Arthur Ty and Toyota Philippines chair Alfred Ty. The FLP Board of Trustees comprises retired Justice Angelina Sandoval-Gutierrez (president), retired Justice Estela M. Perlas-Bernabe, former Education Secretary Edilberto de Jesus, Tetangco, Maria Elena P. Yaptangco, professor Maria Theresa P. Ma├▒alac, lawyers Joel Emerson Gregorio and Arvin Paolo D. Cortez, and CJ as chairperson. In its second decade (2021-2031), FLP aims to set up an immersive, interactive, AI-powered, tech-driven Center for Liberty and Prosperity (in partnership with the Supreme Court) hosting a Museum for Liberty and Prosperity, and a multi-billion-peso Prosperity Fund to help eliminate extreme poverty by investing in MSME equity and helping them through Esmel-fellow management expertise ŌĆö unleashing their entrepreneurial ingenuity. 'To our knowledge, while there are firms engaged in microfinancing, FLP is the only entity aspiring to invest in and help manage MSMEs' ŌĆö making them 'more credit-worthy and growth-worthy.'

one_paragraph_summary: Panganiban responds to CJ Alexander G. Gesmundo's unveiling of the Supreme Court's Strategic Plan for Judicial Innovations 2022-2027 (SPJI) ŌĆö Gesmundo correctly (IMHO) began by recalling CJ Hilario G. Davide Jr.'s 2001 Action Program for Judicial Reform (APJR), 'the first structured plan for judicial reform' from which many SPJI activities were drawn, including technology adoption for court functions, wider public-information reach, and civil-society collaboration. CJ Panganiban dedicated his 536-page 'Judicial Renaissance' (Supreme Court Press, Nov 2006) to CJ Davide, tracing his arc from the remote barangay of Colawin, Argao, Cebu, to lawyer, lawmaker, constitution framer, elections chairman, associate justice, and CJ ŌĆö and documenting APJR's donor support (World Bank, ADB, USAID, CIDA, JICA) with the World Bank featuring the APJR on its website as a model for developing countries. Pre-Davide-retirement on Dec 20, 2005, the Court held the International Conference and Showcase on Judicial Reforms (Nov 28-30, 2005). He thanks Gesmundo for crediting him with 'egalitarian justice' through 'institutionalizing the perspective of law and economics' in the Liberty and Prosperity Programs, then reproduces verbatim his own vision-values-goals statement on assuming office: lead a judiciary of four Ins (integrity, independence, industry, intelligence) morally courageous to resist influence, interference, indifference, and insolence; impervious to the 'plague of ships' (kinship, relationship, friendship, fellowship); continue APJR with focus on four ACID problems (limited access to justice by the poor, corruption, incompetence, delay); seek competent and ethical lawyers; grant maximum benefits to 26,000 employees nationwide in exchange for DHL (dedication, honesty, loyalty); and pursue the two loftier goals of safeguarding liberty and nurturing prosperity under the rule of law. His Oct 18-20, 2006 Global Forum on Liberty and Prosperity capped his incumbency; the post-retirement Foundation for Liberty and Prosperity (FLP) continued the philosophy via professional chairs, legal scholarships, dissertation contests, and Esmel fellowships, with ultimate projects in the Center for Liberty and Prosperity (Museum in SC partnership) and the Entrepreneurship Fund. Gesmundo then hailed CJ Reynato S. Puno as 'father of the great writs of freedom' (Amparo, Habeas Data, Kalikasan), and cited reform contributions of CJ Renato C. Corona (QC Courts; Judicial Affidavit precursor), CJ Teresita Leonardo de Castro (Family Courts; Children in Conflict with the Law), CJ Lucas P. Bersamin (Revised Law Student Practice; Mandatory Clinical Legal Education; 2019 Amendments to the Rules of Civil Procedure; Rules on Evidence), and CJ Diosdado M. Peralta (Continuous Trial 'among others') ŌĆö with the editorial eyebrow-raise 'Sorry, no mention of Maria Lourdes P. A. Sereno's Four Pillars.' Having retired on Dec 7, 2006, CJ closes by acknowledging he doesn't have the details of post-term remedial reforms, referring readers to pio.sc@judiciary.gov.ph.

one_paragraph_summary: Pivoting from his four-part stem-cell series, CJ Panganiban applies the rejuvenation framework to Baguio City. He opens with the personal memory of 1950s-1960s Baguio ├óŌé¼ŌĆØ pine scents, crystal-clear water in Burnham Park, rolling green Mines View mountains 'reminiscent of The Sound of Music' ├óŌé¼ŌĆØ and the half-century collapse into 'obfuscating exhaust... crowded, traffic-choked streets, scalded mountains and murky waters.' Three pieces of good news anchor the recovery thesis. First, Mayor Benjamin Magalong wants to remake Baguio on the Singapore model ├óŌé¼ŌĆØ the only urban site that has, through law and regulation, replicated the longevity outcomes of the rural Blue Zones (Sardinia, Okinawa, Nicoya, Ikaria, Loma Linda) via congestion fees during peak hours, city-spanning trams cheaper than cars, high car taxes capped at one unit per person, tax incentives for green buildings, and mixed-sector apartment structures. Second, BCDA v. CJH Development (April 3, 2024, en banc, 15-0, J Japar B. Dimaampao) upheld the arbitral order evicting CJH Development from a 247-hectare portion of the John Hay Special Economic Zone, with the Court extensively quoting Adapon v. Medical Doctors (June 14, 2021, J Leonen) that arbitral awards cannot be set aside 'for mere errors of judgment either as to the law or as to the facts' ├óŌé¼ŌĆØ affirming arbitration finality as the operative doctrine. Third, the MVP Group, through its subsidiary Landco Pacific Corp., has committed to the interim management of The Manor, Forest Lodge, and the CAMP-John Hay Trade and Cultural Center while BCDA president Joshua M. Bingcang transitions the properties to long-term private partners. CJ closes with the bridging metaphor that ties this piece to the stem-cell series: rejuvenating Baguio is like 'infusing the autologous needles and injecting the living cell therapy into an aging city to rejuvenate, refresh, and reengineer it to a fresh new start. Mabuhay!'

one_paragraph_summary: Panganiban surveys seven little-known 2023 Supreme Court decisions affecting both lawyers and ordinary people. First, Ko v. Republic (April 12, 2023) distinguishes legitimacy (civil status during marriage) from filiation (biological/biotechnological), holding that children conceived or born during marriage are presumed legitimate ŌĆö even when biologically the wife's offspring with another man during separation ŌĆö and the presumption can be challenged only directly (not collaterally) and only by the child, or in some instances by the legal father or his heirs; 'even the mother is prohibited by law from declaring against the child's legitimacy.' Second, Republic v. Pryce (March 8, 2023) extends the senior-citizen 20% discount to interment services including grave-digging, concreting, and other burial services. Third, Ong v. Senate (March 28, 2023) sets out four limitations on congressional legislative-inquiry power ŌĆö in aid of legislation, per published rules, respect for rights of persons, and contempt detention only until end of inquiry ŌĆö and finds the Senate violated the third limitation by holding Pharmally resource persons in contempt without an opportunity to be heard. Fourth, Soliman v. People (April 25, 2023) holds that for online libel (one degree higher than traditional libel under the RPC), courts may impose the alternative penalty of fine instead of imprisonment. Fifth, 4E Steel Builders v. Maybank Philippines (March 13, 2023) sets three cumulative conditions for foreign-bank foreclosure: 5-year maximum possession, no title transfer to the foreign bank, and required transfer to a qualified Philippine national within five years. Sixth, Act Teachers v. Duterte (Jan. 24, 2023) holds that livestream video and photographs of quorum absence cannot prevail over the Congressional Journal because 'no less than the Constitution itself grants the Congressional Journal its imprimatur' ŌĆö to which CJ adds his humble opinion that the separation of powers grants each branch autonomy over internal processes (SC respects the Journal; Congress respects SC internal deliberations and votes; both respect Executive Cabinet internal decisions). Seventh, People v. Almayda (June 14, 2023) holds that in warrantless drug seizures, physical inventory and photo-taking must as a rule be done at the place of seizure, with exception only on 'clear, consistent, and sensible affidavits' showing impracticability or immediate/extreme danger at the seizure site.

one_paragraph_summary: Panganiban analyzes the Court of Appeals' decision in Chiong v. Anonymous MIAA Officials (March 21, 2024, J Eleuterio L. Bathan, 13th Division) reversing the Office of the Ombudsman's Aug 1, 2023 dismissal of MIAA acting general manager Cesar M. Chiong and acting assistant general manager Irene P. Montalbo for grave misconduct, abuse of authority/oppression, and conduct prejudicial to the best interest of the service (with forfeiture of all retirement benefits and perpetual disqualification). Consistent with the new Supreme Court stylebook, the CA gave two main reasons: (1) the Civil Service Commission, not the OMB, has primary jurisdiction to determine the legality of the reassignment of 285 MIAA employees, so absent a prior CSC ruling the OMB decision was 'struck down for prematurity'; (2) even granting an independent OMB examination, the verdict lacked factual basis and substantial-evidence support ŌĆö the OMB 'merely made a general statement.' He restates the OMB's Article XI Section 5 plenary power as 'protector of the people' to investigate any act 'illegal, unjust, improper or inefficient,' but stresses it must respect respondents' constitutional rights AND the powers of coequal independent agencies like the CSC; OMB administrative actions are appealable to the CA, criminal cases go to the Sandiganbayan, and direct SC recourse is available on constitutional or grave-abuse grounds (especially for OMB delay, on which CJ refuses to repeat himself). He then offers an extended tribute to Justice Conchita Carpio Morales as 'perhaps the most celebrated' OMB ŌĆö surprise-appointed by President Aquino during his July 25, 2011 SONA a month after her June 19 SC retirement, and notable as the first woman to administer the oath of office to a president (Aquino) and a vice president (Binay) on June 30, 2010. During her 2011-2018 term she relentlessly filed cases against former president Arroyo (who had named her associate justice), former VP Binay, and senators Enrile, Revilla, and Estrada for nonbailable offenses resulting in pretrial detention. Admirers crowned her (with then-DOJ Sec. Leila de Lima and COA Chair Grace Pulido-Tan) as the 'Three Furies' and the Inquirer's 2014 'Filipino of the Year'; UP gave her an honorary doctorate; she received the 2016 Ramon Magsaysay Award ('Asia's Nobel Prize'). To be fair, critics fault her 'allegedly baseless' charges against officials later acquitted or released on bail after many years of detention. 'A hero to many and a heel to others,' she continues post-retirement advocacy ŌĆö including filing with late SOFA Albert del Rosario an ICC communication against Chinese President Xi Jinping for China's environmentally destructive SCS activities, exhibiting 'indefatigable stamina' for good governance.

one_paragraph_summary: Inverting the usual stereotype, Panganiban argues that the Philippine Supreme Court and the Catholic Church ŌĆö traditional bulwarks of conservatism ŌĆö are moving progressively on a 'rocky road to liberalism,' while the US Supreme Court (Scotus), traditional bastion of liberalism, has turned conservative. The PH SC's liberal trek is illustrated by Republic v Ng (Feb 27, 2024, J Dimaampao, en banc, 13-2), which recognized foreign divorces ŌĆö even by administrative process or mutual agreement (as practiced in Japan and Korea) ŌĆö by reading Article 26(2) of the Family Code according to its spirit and intent (sparing Filipino spouses from disadvantage when foreign spouses are already free to remarry), and by Pulido v People (July 27, 2021, J Hernando, en banc, 15-0), which acquitted an accused of bigamy even without a judicial declaration of nullity of a void-ab-initio first marriage (though judicial declaration is still needed for remarriage and civil-rights questions like heirship). CJ is surprised that 13 insider-justices trended liberal, with only two outsiders (UP, DOJ) predictably liberal. The Catholic Church has likewise been liberalizing ŌĆö Vatican II (1962-1965) made it more humane and ecumenical (vernacular masses, priests facing the faithful), PCP2 implemented this in the Philippines, Pope John Paul II carried it forward, and Pope Francis as the first non-European pontiff is even more determined; his Sept 13 Singapore extemporaneous remarks ('All religions are paths to reach GodŌĆ” God is God for all, then we are all children of God') will 'rock the rafters' and intensify traditionalist opposition. By contrast, Scotus tipped from 5-4 to 6-3 conservative with Trump's three appointees (Gorsuch, Kavanaugh, Barrett), and Dobbs v Jackson (June 24, 2022) reversed Roe v Wade (Jan 22, 1973). Combined with immigration, democracy, jobs, and the economy, abortion has turned the Nov 5 Harris-Trump contest into 'acrimonious combat between liberals and conservatives' whose ramifications will shake the wars in Ukraine, Gaza, and Lebanon, the tensions in the North and South China Sea, and the Israel-Iran standoff. CJ's fervent hope is that liberalism not become 'licentiousness' subverting the rule of law, and that conservatism not become Hitler-style Nazism violating individual liberty ŌĆö but he is 'terrified by the rants of Trump that, if elected, he would become a dictator. Heaven forbid!'

one_paragraph_summary: A reader observed that the prior Monday's column called the road to liberalism of the Supreme Court and the Catholic Church 'rocky' yet gave only easy examples (15-0 and 13-2). CJ accepts the correction and offers a genuinely rocky case: XXX v People (April 16, 2024, J Hernando, en banc, 9-5-1) ruled that marital infidelity causing mental or emotional anguish is psychological violence punishable with imprisonment and fine under Section 5(i) of RA 9262. The majority held that the specific intent to cause anguish 'may be conclusively presumed from the fact of infidelity itself' ŌĆö even casual or infrequent ŌĆö with Justices Lazaro-Javier and Singh writing compelling concurrences. The minority of five contended that criminal intent had not been proven and is looking for another similar case to prevail when the Court's composition changes. CJ's simple advice to husbands: infidelity is 'dangerous to life and liberty' ŌĆö though RA 9262 punishes only philandering husbands and is sub-silentio on philandering wives. On abortion, hot in the US presidential campaign, CJ notes the battle is pass├® in the Philippines because abortion is barred by the Constitution and criminalized by law; the only question is when life begins. Imbong v Ochoa (April 9, 2014, J Mendoza, en banc, 11-1-3, 106 pages) ruled that 'a zygote is a human organism' and 'the life of a new human being commencesŌĆ” upon fertilization,' explicitly rejecting Rep. Edcel Lagman's implantation theory; constitutional framers Bernardo M. Villegas and (later CJ) Hilario G. Davide Jr. had stressed protection 'from the moment of conception,' and the right to life is 'grounded on natural law,' inherent and not a creation of statute. Consequently, health professionals cannot be forced to use post-fertilization devices or drugs; some theologians prescribe only the 'rhythm method.' On PCP2, CJ disclaims canon-law expertise but quotes Article 28: lay men and women in responsible positions must help form the civic conscience and promote leaders of true integrity, while bishops, priests, and religious must refrain from partisan politics ŌĆö especially from using the pulpit for partisan purposes ŌĆö per Canons 287 #2 and 672. Further questions, he says, may be addressed to a canon lawyer or by buying the P308 paperback of the 'Acts and Decrees' from St. Paul's Superstore in San Antonio Village, Makati.
